html:
[201]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 相与谈论 - To converse together, a common expression for engaging in dialogue.
  • 慧舌生香 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the words are so wise and eloquent that they seem to emit a fragrance.'
  • 藻思耀采 - The thoughts are so rich and the expressions are so brilliant, describing the beauty and depth of the conversation.
  • 邬爱且服 - Wu loves and admires, indicating a strong emotional and intellectual connection.
  • 听玉漏丁丁 - Listening to the sound of the jade drip (a type of water clock), a poetic way to describe the passage of time.
  • 墙外之柝四下 - The watchman's drum beats four times outside the wall, indicating the time of night.
  • 促其解衣 - Urge him to take off his clothes, a polite way to suggest intimacy.
  • 飞红上颊 - Blush rises to the cheeks, a vivid description of embarrassment or excitement.
  • 约以明宵 - Agree to meet again the next night, a promise for a future encounter.
  • 野鸡四啼 - The roosters crow four times, another way to indicate the time of night.
  • 仓皇遽遁 - Flee in haste, describing a hurried departure.
  • 翌果挑灯自携衾枕至 - The next day, she indeed brings a lamp and her bedding, indicating preparation for intimacy.
  • 备极华丽 - Extremely luxurious, describing the opulence of the bedding.
  • 真犹处子 - Truly like a virgin, emphasizing the purity of the encounter.
  • 臂代枕 - Using her arm as a pillow, a romantic gesture.
  • 口吟一词 - Reciting a poem, a form of intimate expression.
  • 郎可怜，妾可怜 - Dear, I pity you; Dear, I pity me, expressing mutual affection and concern.
  • 一对鸳鸯一对鹣 - A pair of mandarin ducks and a pair of jia, a metaphor for a loving couple.
  • 今宵那世缘 - Tonight, our worldly缘分 (fate), expressing the significance of the moment.
  • 莫流连 - Do not linger, a warning to avoid excessive attachment.
  • 且流连 - Yet linger, a contradictory instruction that adds complexity to the advice.
  • 生怕钟鸣欲曙天 - Afraid that the bell will ring and dawn will break, expressing fear of the end of the night.
  • 情人隔一边 - Lovers separated by one side, a poignant expression of distance.
  • 风谁家，月谁家 - Whose is the wind? Whose is the moon? a rhetorical question expressing the universality of love.
  • 妾岂当门卖笑娃 - I am not a courtesan selling smiles at the door, emphasizing the sincerity of her feelings.
  • 情深念转差 - Deep feelings turn into thoughts of difference, expressing the complexity of emotions.
  • 香辟邪，正辟邪 - Pungent to ward off evil, correct to ward off evil, a metaphor for the protective power of love.
  • 夜雨摧残一树花 - Night rain damages a tree of flowers, a metaphor for the fragility of love.
  • 郎君郑重些 - Dear, be serious, a request for respect and care.
  • 自摘耳上两金环 - She herself picks off the two gold rings from her ears, a gesture of commitment.
  • 以此作定情物 - Use this as a token of love, a formal way to establish a relationship.
  • 慎勿示人 - Be careful not to show it to others, a warning to keep the relationship private.
  • 恐飞短流长 - Fear that rumors will spread, expressing concern about gossip.
  • 彼此不利 - Harm both of us, indicating the potential consequences of gossip.
  • 自此来无虚夕 - From then on, there was no empty night, indicating a regular pattern of meetings.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 邬爱且服 - Refers to the character Wu, who is deeply impressed by the other character.
  • 玉漏丁丁 - Refers to the jade drip, a type of water clock used in ancient China to measure time.
  • 墙外之柝 - Refers to the watchman's drum, a traditional method of signaling the time of night.
  • 野鸡四啼 - Refers to the rooster's crow, a common way to indicate the time of night.
  • 翌果挑灯自携衾枕至 - The next day, she indeed brings a lamp and her bedding, indicating preparation for intimacy.
  • 备极华丽 - Extremely luxurious, describing the opulence of the bedding.
  • 真犹处子 - Truly like a virgin, emphasizing the purity of the encounter.
  • 臂代枕 - Using her arm as a pillow, a romantic gesture.
  • 口吟一词 - Reciting a poem, a form of intimate expression.
  • 郎可怜，妾可怜 - Dear, I pity you; Dear, I pity me, expressing mutual affection and concern.
  • 一对鸳鸯一对鹣 - A pair of mandarin ducks and a pair of jia, a metaphor for a loving couple.
  • 今宵那世缘 - Tonight, our worldly缘分 (fate), expressing the significance of the moment.
  • 莫流连 - Do not linger, a warning to avoid excessive attachment.
  • 且流连 - Yet linger, a contradictory instruction that adds complexity to the advice.
  • 生怕钟鸣欲曙天 - Afraid that the bell will ring and dawn will break, expressing fear of the end of the night.
  • 情人隔一边 - Lovers separated by one side, a poignant expression of distance.
  • 风谁家，月谁家 - Whose is the wind? Whose is the moon? a rhetorical question expressing the universality of love.
  • 妾岂当门卖笑娃 - I am not a courtesan selling smiles at the door, emphasizing the sincerity of her feelings.
  • 情深念转差 - Deep feelings turn into thoughts of difference, expressing the complexity of emotions.
  • 香辟邪，正辟邪 - Pungent to ward off evil, correct to ward off evil, a metaphor for the protective power of love.
  • 夜雨摧残一树花 - Night rain damages a tree of flowers, a metaphor for the fragility of love.
  • 郎君郑重些 - Dear, be serious, a request for respect and care.
  • 自摘耳上两金环 - She herself picks off the two gold rings from her ears, a gesture of commitment.
  • 以此作定情物 - Use this as a token of love, a formal way to establish a relationship.
  • 慎勿示人 - Be careful not to show it to others, a warning to keep the relationship private.
  • 恐飞短流长 - Fear that rumors will spread, expressing concern about gossip.
  • 彼此不利 - Harm both of us, indicating the potential consequences of gossip.
  • 自此来无虚夕 - From then on, there was no empty night, indicating a regular pattern of meetings.

[202]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夜正偎拥 - Literally means 'one night, they were hugging each other closely,' describing an intimate moment.
  • 斑白叟 - Refers to an elderly man with white hair, often used to denote a wise or authoritative figure.
  • 面靛裂，发蓬飞 - Describes a man with a disheveled appearance, his face distorted with rage, and his hair flying wildly.
  • 霜髯如戟 - A vivid metaphor comparing the old man's beard to spears, emphasizing his fierce and intimidating presence.
  • 小妮子，太不识羞耻 - A harsh rebuke, with '小妮子' referring to a young woman in a derogatory manner and '不识羞耻' meaning 'lacking shame.'
  • 污人清白 - Means 'to tarnish someone's purity or innocence.'
  • 风狂儿不当杀却耶 - A rhetorical question expressing the old man's anger, with '风狂儿' referring to the young man and '杀却' meaning 'to kill.'
  • 惊惶无地 - Means 'extremely frightened and at a loss.'
  • 口噤不能言 - Describes being unable to speak, with '口噤' meaning 'mute' or 'speechless.'
  • 齿牙震击作奇响 - Means 'the teeth and jaw clattering in a strange sound,' indicating extreme fear or shock.
  • 被隙微窥 - Means 'peeking through the gap in the blanket,' describing a secretive or cautious action.
  • 觳觫可怜 - Means 'trembling with fear, pitiful,' describing the young woman's state.
  • 呵斥益厉 - Means 'scolding even more fiercely.'
  • 仆在外舍 - Means 'falling outside the room,' describing a physical collapse.
  • 反侧匡床间 - Means 'lying restlessly on the bed,' describing an agitated state.
  • 次夜，邬扃户 - Means 'the next night, Wu locked the door,' with '扃户' meaning 'locking the door.'
  • 眠不熟 - Means 'unable to sleep soundly.'
  • 袅娜在床侧 - Describes the young woman's graceful and delicate presence beside the bed.
  • 娇羞惨淡 - Means 'shyly and sadly,' describing her emotional state.
  • 默无一言 - Means 'silent and without a word.'
  • 执其手 - Means 'holding her hand,' a gesture of affection or comfort.
  • 老叟属卿何人 - A question asking who the old man is related to, with '属' meaning 'belong to' or 'be related to.'
  • 老父也 - Means 'my father,' revealing the identity of the old man.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 斑白叟 - In classical Chinese literature, an elderly man with white hair often symbolizes wisdom, authority, or even supernatural power.
  • 面靛裂，发蓬飞，霜髯如戟 - These descriptions are typical of characters in classical literature who are depicted as fierce, authoritative, or even demonic.
  • 小妮子 - A term used to refer to a young woman, often with a derogatory or dismissive connotation.
  • 不识羞耻 - A phrase used to criticize someone for lacking moral integrity or shame.
  • 污人清白 - A serious accusation in classical Chinese culture, implying that someone's reputation or purity has been tarnished.
  • 风狂儿 - A term used to describe someone who is reckless or out of control, often with a negative connotation.
  • 杀却 - A strong term meaning 'to kill,' often used in rhetorical questions to express extreme anger or frustration.
  • 惊惶无地 - A phrase describing extreme fear and confusion, often used to depict a character's state of mind in moments of crisis.
  • 口噤不能言 - A common expression in classical literature to describe someone who is so shocked or frightened that they cannot speak.
  • 齿牙震击作奇响 - A vivid description of someone's physical reaction to extreme fear or shock.
  • 被隙微窥 - A secretive or cautious action, often used to describe someone who is trying to observe something without being noticed.
  • 觳觫可怜 - A phrase describing someone who is trembling with fear, often used to evoke sympathy for a character.
  • 呵斥益厉 - A phrase describing someone who is scolding or rebuking others with increasing intensity.
  • 仆在外舍 - A phrase describing someone who has collapsed or fallen outside a room, often used to depict a physical or emotional breakdown.
  • 反侧匡床间 - A phrase describing someone who is lying restlessly on a bed, often used to depict agitation or anxiety.
  • 次夜，邬扃户 - A phrase describing the next night, with '邬' referring to the young man and '扃户' meaning 'locking the door.'
  • 眠不熟 - A phrase describing someone who is unable to sleep soundly, often used to depict restlessness or anxiety.
  • 袅娜在床侧 - A phrase describing someone's graceful and delicate presence beside the bed, often used to depict a young woman's beauty or elegance.
  • 娇羞惨淡 - A phrase describing someone who is shyly and sadly, often used to depict a young woman's emotional state.
  • 默无一言 - A phrase describing someone who is silent and without a word, often used to depict a character's state of mind in moments of shock or confusion.
  • 执其手 - A gesture of affection or comfort, often used to depict a character's emotional connection to another.
  • 老叟属卿何人 - A question asking who the old man is related to, often used to reveal a character's identity or relationship.
  • 老父也 - A phrase revealing the identity of the old man as the young woman's father.

Proper Nouns

  • 邬 - The name of the young man.
  • 女 - The name of the young woman.

[203]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 曰 - Quotative particle meaning 'said' or 'spoke', used to introduce dialogue.
  • 卿家大人 - Respectful term meaning 'your esteemed father' or 'your honored father'.
  • 匝月恩爱 - Literally 'a full month of love', referring to a period of intense affection.
  • 玉台艳偶 - Poetic expression meaning 'lovely match' or 'beautiful partner', often used to describe a desirable spouse.
  • 犯险阻争异类 - Literally 'risk danger and struggle against different kinds', implying taking risks to win the affection of someone outside one's usual social circle.
  • 家君素严 - Means 'my father is usually strict', indicating a strict household.
  • 翌即迁他郡 - Means 'the next day he will move to another prefecture', referring to a planned relocation.
  • 永辞 - Means 'farewell forever' or 'parting permanently'.
  • 袖中红巾 - Refers to a red handkerchief in the sleeve, a common item used for wiping tears.
  • 重物 - Means 'valuable item' or 'precious thing', referring to something of significant worth.
  • 天如鉴怜 - Phrase invoking divine judgment and compassion, meaning 'may heaven judge and pity'.
  • 镜可圆而剑可合 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that things can be restored to their original state, often used in the context of reunions or reconciliation.
  • 顿杳 - Means 'suddenly disappeared' or 'vanished abruptly'.
  • 修竹风敲 - Describes the sound of bamboo rustling in the wind, a poetic image often used to evoke a serene or melancholic atmosphere.
  • 玉体羸败 - Means 'body weakened and frail', describing a state of physical decline.
  • 玉人 - Term of endearment meaning 'beautiful person' or 'beloved'.
  • 阿翠 - The name of a female fortune-teller or sorceress.
  • 目能见狐 - Means 'can see foxes with her eyes', indicating supernatural abilities.
  • 知狐所在 - Means 'knows where the fox is', referring to the ability to locate a specific fox.
  • 若其好着淡黄帔薄罗衫，面团团如月，一笑两颊上生微涡者耶 - Detailed description of the fox's appearance, meaning 'if it is the one who likes to wear light yellow robes and thin silk shirts, with a round face like the moon, and two dimples appear on her cheeks when she smiles?'
  • 非他，骆氏小素也 - Means 'it is not someone else, it is Xiaosu of the Luo family', identifying the fox by name.
  • 阿翠请生作简，愿任作寄书邮 - Means 'Aici asks him to write a letter, and she is willing to deliver it', indicating Aici's offer to act as a messenger.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - Refers to a fox, often associated with supernatural beings in Chinese folklore, particularly fox spirits (huli jing) known for their beauty and seductive powers.
  • 玉台 - Term often used in classical literature to refer to a high-ranking official's residence or a place of beauty and elegance.
  • 玉人 - Term of endearment, often used to describe a beautiful and beloved person.
  • 镜 - The mirror is a symbol of clarity and reflection, often used in stories to represent the restoration of relationships or the revelation of truth.
  • 剑 - The sword is a symbol of strength and honor, often used in stories to represent the restoration of relationships or the resolution of conflicts.
  • 阿翠 - Female fortune-teller or sorceress, a common figure in Chinese folklore and literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿翠 - The name of a female fortune-teller or sorceress.
  • 骆氏小素 - The name of the fox, identified as Xiaosu of the Luo family.

[204]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 数日来报 - Formal expression meaning 'reports came after several days.'
  • 小素匆促，不及裁笺 - Literally 'Xiaosu was in a hurry and did not have time to write a letter.' Common way to express inability to write a formal letter due to time constraints.
  • 着传语奉复郎君 - Literally 'instructing to convey a message to the young master,' indicating message conveyed through a third party.
  • 前实缘尽 - Literally 'the previous connection was indeed severed,' suggesting end of a relationship or bond.
  • 恐径自别去 - Literally 'fearing that she would leave directly,' implying fear of abrupt separation.
  • 苦郎相思 - Literally 'making the young master suffer from longing,' indicating emotional distress.
  • 幻此形状 - Literally 'creating this form,' suggesting supernatural or magical transformation.
  • 俾郎君心死 - Literally 'making the young master's heart die,' implying desire to end young master's feelings of love.
  • 乘便寄丹砂一粒 - Literally 'taking the opportunity to send a piece of cinnabar,' indicating small gift sent along with message.
  • 可以却病痛 - Literally 'can dispel illness and pain,' suggesting medicinal properties of cinnabar.
  • 邬视药小而红，香甚 - Literally 'Wu saw the medicine small and red, very fragrant,' describing appearance and scent of cinnabar.
  • 一服，疾果瘳 - Literally 'after taking it once, the illness indeed disappeared,' indicating effectiveness of medicine.
  • 而思女之心亦释 - Literally 'and the heart's longing for the woman was also released,' indicating release of emotional distress.
  • 懊侬氏曰 - Formal way of introducing a commentator or author, similar to 'the author says' or 'the commentator remarks.'
  • 情之所在 - Literally 'where emotion lies,' emphasizing power and influence of emotion.
  • 父母师保不能止 - Literally 'parents, teachers, and guardians cannot stop it,' indicating irresistible nature of emotion.
  • 天地鬼神不能禁 - Literally 'heaven and earth, gods and spirits cannot forbid it,' further emphasizing overwhelming power of emotion.
  • 山川河海不能隔 - Literally 'mountains, rivers, seas, and oceans cannot separate it,' indicating boundless reach of emotion.
  • 顾为情而来，情未尽，则麾之不去 - Literally 'because of emotion, if the emotion is not exhausted, it cannot be dismissed,' suggesting true emotion persists until fully resolved.
  • 情既尽，即招之不来 - Literally 'if the emotion is exhausted, it cannot be called back,' indicating once emotion is resolved, it cannot be rekindled.
  • 不以余情害情人 - Literally 'not harming the beloved with leftover emotion,' suggesting selfless approach to love.
  • 复能以幻相警痴子 - Literally 'and can use a magical form to warn the foolish,' indicating supernatural intervention to protect beloved.
  • 是非真深于情者乎 - Literally 'is this not truly deep in emotion?' questioning depth and sincerity of emotion.
  • 否则，朝伐夕戕，非髓竭神枯而不已 - Literally 'otherwise, morning attacks and evening injuries, not stopping until the marrow is exhausted and the spirit is dried up,' indicating destructive nature of unrequited love.
  • 又或洁身而退，令人魂销气结不能忘，真冤且孽矣 - Literally 'or else, withdrawing oneself, making others' souls and spirits break and unable to forget, truly a grudge and a curse,' indicating lasting impact of unrequited love.
  • 尚得谓之情乎 - Literally 'can this still be called emotion?' questioning validity of such intense but ultimately unfulfilled emotion.
  • 如小素者，方可与言情 - Literally 'like Xiaosu, one can speak of emotion,' suggesting only those who understand true nature of emotion can truly discuss it.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 小素 - Fictional character, likely a woman, subject of the story.
  • 邬 - Character who receives message and cinnabar.
  • 丹砂 - Cinnabar, red mineral used in traditional Chinese medicine and alchemy, believed to have healing properties.
  • 情 - Emotion, particularly romantic love, central theme in the text.
  • 幻相 - Supernatural or magical form, suggesting transformation or illusion.
  • 懊侬氏 - Commentator or author, possibly pseudonym, who provides philosophical reflection on nature of emotion.

Proper Nouns

  • 小素 - Fictional character.
  • 邬 - Character who receives message and cinnabar.
  • 丹砂 - Cinnabar, red mineral.
  • 懊侬氏 - Commentator or author.

[205]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 假五通神 - Refers to the veneration of the Five Lords (五通神), who are deities in Chinese folk religion. '假' here means 'to pretend' or 'to feign,' suggesting a pretense of worship.
  • 崇奉 - Means 'to worship' or 'to venerate,' indicating a strong devotion to a deity.
  • 信狐 - A reference to the belief in fox spirits (狐仙) in Chinese folklore, which is common in northern China.
  • 阿芙蓉 - Refers to opium (阿片), a substance derived from the opium poppy.
  • 发籍 - Means 'to distribute' or 'to sell,' referring to the act of selling opium.
  • 齐楚间 - Refers to the region between Qi (齐) and Chu (楚), two ancient states in China, indicating a specific geographical area.
  • 纳粟为九品官 - Means 'to buy a ninth-rank official position with grain,' a reference to the practice of purchasing official positions in ancient China.
  • 在籍候铨 - Means 'to be on the list awaiting appointment,' indicating a state of being registered for an official position.
  • 煌煌章服 - Refers to splendid official robes, symbolizing high rank and status.
  • 腰佩玉 - Means 'to wear a jade pendant around the waist,' a sign of wealth and refinement.
  • 腕跳脱 - Refers to bracelets or bangles worn on the wrists.
  • 襟洋表 - Means 'to wear a Western-style watch in the pocket,' indicating a blend of traditional and modern elements.
  • 面架墨晶镜 - Refers to wearing dark crystal glasses, a fashionable accessory.
  • 烟霞营生 - Means 'to make a living from the smoke and clouds,' a euphemism for dealing in opium.
  • 弃隶学贾 - Means 'to abandon a minor official position to learn commerce,' indicating a career change.
  • 精歌唱丝竹等艺 - Refers to being skilled in singing, playing stringed instruments, and other arts.
  • 日诱良家子作狭斜游 - Means 'to daily lure well-born young men into the demimonde,' referring to leading them into a life of debauchery.
  • 多外宿不归 - Means 'to often stay out overnight and not return home,' indicating a life of promiscuity and disregard for family.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五通神 - The Five Lords are deities in Chinese folk religion, often associated with wealth and protection but also with mischief and immorality.
  • 阿芙蓉 - Opium was a significant commodity in China during the 19th century, leading to widespread addiction and social problems.
  • 齐楚间 - The region between Qi and Chu was historically significant in Chinese history, known for its cultural and political importance.
  • 纳粟为九品官 - The practice of buying official positions with grain was a form of corruption in ancient China.
  • 在籍候铨 - This refers to the bureaucratic system where individuals were registered for official positions and awaited appointment.
  • 烟霞营生 - This phrase is a euphemism for dealing in opium, which was a major source of income for many in the 19th century.
  • 狭斜游 - Refers to the demimonde, a world of vice and debauchery, often associated with prostitution and gambling.

Proper Nouns

  • 万 - The surname of the character.
  • 佳儿 - The character's childhood name.
  • 佳 - The character's given name.
  • 颗珠 - The character's courtesy name.
  • 雍 - The character's wife's surname.

[206]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雍独居 - 雍独自居住
  • 挑灯凝睇 - 挑灯凝视
  • 对镜颐 - 对着镜子自言自语
  • 若有男子影 - 似乎有男子的影子
  • 心疑夫婿潜回斗笑 - 心中怀疑丈夫悄悄回来嘲笑
  • 喜而回顾 - 高兴地回头
  • 翩翩美少年 - 翩翩英俊的少年
  • 体态妍丽 - 体态美丽
  • 羊车中人 - 羊车中的人
  • 乃生平所绝未邂逅者也 - 是我一生中从未见过的
  • 惊欲诘 - 惊讶地想要询问
  • 觉口噤神迷 - 感觉嘴巴紧闭，神志迷糊
  • 细腻熨贴 - 细腻贴切
  • 慰藉良殷 - 安慰非常殷切
  • 自陈 - 自我陈述
  • 为五通四郎 - 我是五通四郎
  • 怜子孤衾 - 怜悯你孤独的被褥
  • 特来相伴 - 特地来陪伴
  • 未知卿卿亦如小生之痴情否 - 不知道你是否也像我一样痴情
  • 欲撑拒 - 想要抵抗
  • 觉四郎肌肤中有一缕幽香 - 感觉四郎的肌肤中有一缕幽香
  • 透入鼻观 - 透入鼻中
  • 又舌入己口 - 舌头又进入自己的口中
  • 搅扰吮咂 - 搅扰吮吸
  • 心遂大动 - 心顿时激动
  • 听其所为 - 听任他的行为
  • 事讫 - 事情结束后
  • 神顿清 - 神志顿时清醒
  • 仓皇窜匿 - 仓皇逃跑躲藏
  • 怒抽壁上刀挥之 - 愤怒地抽出墙上的刀砍向他
  • 遽化白光一线 - 立即变成一线白光
  • 飕然从窗缝中出 - 嗖的一声从窗缝中出去
  • 问妇 - 询问妻子
  • 妇诡云 - 妻子诡秘地说
  • 无所见 - 什么也没看见

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 五通四郎 - Wutong Siling (a supernatural being in Chinese folklore, often associated with love and seduction)
  • 孤衾 - lonely bed (metaphorically referring to a lonely or solitary state)
  • 羊车中人 - person in a sheep-drawn carriage (a metaphor for a beautiful or graceful woman, often used in classical literature)

Proper Nouns

  • 雍 - The protagonist of the story
  • 五通四郎 - The supernatural being who visits雍
  • 佳偶 - The wife of雍

[207]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然枕席上竟由此大冷落，无腻语，无欢颜 - Describes a sudden change from intimacy to coldness and silence.
  • 四郎又乘间来 - "乘间" means "taking advantage of an opportunity" or "when unobserved."
  • 奴方期图永好，而今已矣 - "期图" means "to hope for" or "to plan for," and "永好" means "eternal happiness."

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 好勤職，報君王 - Moral exhortation meaning 'to diligently perform one’s duties in service to the king' or 'to be diligent in one’s post in order to serve the king.'
  • 毋贾祸，累高堂 - Cautionary phrase meaning 'do not bring calamity upon yourself, and burden your parents (or family).' '贾祸' is a classical expression meaning 'to invite disaster' or 'to bring about calamity.'
  • 刺已，以朱涅之，深入肌里 - '刺已' is a verb phrase meaning 'to prick oneself' or 'to pierce the body.' '以朱涅之' means 'to mark with vermilion' or 'to stain with red dye.' This is likely a ritual or symbolic act, possibly for protection or purification.
  • 徒步行 - Literally 'to walk on foot,' indicating a journey without horses or other conveyances.
  • 逾武定，近德州界 - '逾' means 'to cross over' or 'to pass through.' '武定' is likely a place name (Wuding), and '德州' is Dezhou, a city in Shandong. So the phrase means 'crossing Wuding, approaching the borders of Dezhou.'
  • 山雨暴至 - 'Sudden mountain rain' or 'a sudden downpour in the mountains,' indicating a sudden and heavy storm.
  • 装负淋漓 - '装负' means 'clothing and burden' or 'clothes and load,' and '淋漓' means 'soaked' or 'drenched.' So the phrase means 'clothes and belongings soaked through.'
  • 瞰岭后有古精蓝 - '瞰' means 'to look down upon' or 'to survey.' '岭' is a hill or mountain ridge. '古精蓝' is an archaic term for an ancient Buddhist monastery or temple.
  • 意待霁而后行 - '意' is 'intending' or 'planning to.' '霁' means 'clear weather after rain.' So the phrase means 'intending to wait for the rain to clear before continuing the journey.'
  • 久之日暮，雨淅沥如故 - '久之' is a time phrase meaning 'after a long while.' '日暮' is 'sunset' or 'evening.' '雨淅沥如故' means 'the rain still drizzled on as before.'
  • 遂决意止宿 - '遂' is 'then' or 'so.' '决意' is 'to resolve firmly.' '止宿' is 'to stop and stay for the night.'
  • 视庙中断井颓垣 - '视' is 'to look at.' '断井颓垣' is 'broken wells and crumbling walls,' indicating a ruined temple.
  • 神鬼露处 - '神鬼' is 'gods and ghosts.' '露处' is 'exposed to the elements' or 'left in the open.'
  • 惟中殿尚能避风雨 - '惟' is 'only' or 'only the.' '中殿' is 'central hall' or 'main hall.' '尚能避风雨' is 'still able to shelter from wind and rain.'
  • 香案尘渍，杂蜡泪 - '香案' is an incense table or altar. '尘渍' is 'dust and stains.' '杂蜡泪' is 'mixed with wax tears' (from candles).
  • 抽刀铲削，以案作榻 - '抽刀' is 'to draw a blade' or 'to take a knife.' '铲削' is 'to scrape and chisel.' '以案作榻' is 'to use the altar as a bed.'
  • 遂对神再拜，祝而高坐 - '遂' is 'then.' '对神再拜' is 'to bow twice before the deity.' '祝而高坐' is 'to make a vow and then sit high' or 'to pray and then sit in a high position.'
  • 咽囊中干糇 - '咽' is 'to swallow.' '囊中干糇' is 'dried food in a bag' or 'dry provisions in a pouch.'

  • 两廊鬼判欲攫人 - '两廊' is 'the two corridors' or 'the sides of the hall.' '鬼判' is 'ghost judges' or 'spectral officials.' '欲攫人' is 'wanting to seize a person.'
  • 心亦不惧 - '心亦不惧' is 'the heart also did not fear.'
  • 甫再交睫，闻声 - '甫' is 'just' or 'only just.' '再交睫' is 'to close one’s eyes again' or 'to blink twice.' '闻' is 'to hear.' '声' is an unusual character, likely representing a sound, possibly a soft, rustling or whispering noise.
  • 睨之，一二八好女子 - '睨之' is 'glanced at it.' '一二八' is likely a numerical description, possibly 'a girl of 18' or 'a woman of 18 years.' '好女子' is 'a beautiful woman.'
  • 妆虽不整，貌惨淡，衣朴素 - '妆虽不整' is 'her makeup was not properly done.' '貌惨淡' is 'her appearance was sad or gloomy.' '衣朴素' is 'her clothes were plain.'
  • 五官位置极丽端 - '五官' is 'five features' (eyes, nose, mouth, etc.). '位置极丽端' is 'arranged in a very beautiful and proper way.'

  • 饮已，跪伏神座下，喃喃不能辨 - '饮已' is 'after drinking.' '跪伏' is 'to kneel and crouch.' '神座' is 'the deity’s seat' or 'the altar.' '喃喃不能辨' is 'murmuring in a way that could not be understood.'
  • 郝毛发森竖，意为鬼魅无疑 - '郝' is likely a name (possibly 'Hao') or a typo for '毛发森竖' (hair stands on end). '意为鬼魅无疑' is 'intended that it was definitely a ghost or demon.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刺已，以朱涅之 - This is likely a ritual or symbolic act of self-marking with vermilion. Similar practices are found in ancient Chinese rites of protection or purification.
  • 精蓝 - An archaic term for a Buddhist monastery or temple. This suggests a setting in a religious or monastic context.
  • 神鬼 - 'Gods and ghosts' reflects the syncretic religious worldview in classical China, where the supernatural was often part of daily life.
  • 香案尘渍，杂蜡泪 - This indicates that the temple had been long abandoned and neglected, with the altar covered in dust and candle wax.
  • 祝而高坐 - A ritual act of prayer or vow, followed by a symbolic act of sitting in a high place, possibly to assert authority or spiritual presence.
  • 干糇 - A term for dry provisions or dry food.

  • 郎又娶得床头生菩萨第三尊 - Metaphorical expression where '床头生菩萨' (a living Buddha by the bed) refers to a virtuous and beautiful wife, and '第三尊' (third one) indicates the third such wife.
  • 不怕醋瓶倒耶 - Playful reference to jealousy, suggesting that the husband is not afraid of being 'overwhelmed' by jealousy.
  • 生以二娘贤淑告媪 - The young man told the old woman about the second wife's virtue and beauty.
  • 陶公祠畔，枣花门内，新来江南白妪，携小娇生四名，均钱树子 - Detailed description of a new arrival: 'At the side of the Taoist temple, inside the gate with red flowers, a new old woman from Jiangnan, carrying four young and beautiful girls, all of whom are like money trees.'
  • 第一为西贾攫去 - The first one was taken away by a Western merchant, where '西贾' (Western merchant) likely refers to a wealthy or influential person.
  • 第四美尤冠 - The fourth one is the most beautiful, emphasizing her exceptional beauty.
  • 老身请为郎执鞭 - Humble and respectful way to offer service, meaning 'I will serve the young man as a servant.'
  • 牵珠帘，拉生入，诸婢含笑迓客 - She pulled the pearl curtain, led the young man in, and the maids greeted him with smiles.
  • 鸦髻绿裤摺，已觉可人 - She had a crow-like bun and green pleated pants, which already made her endearing.
  • 姆即前云大钱神耶 - Is she the big money goddess mentioned before?
  • 恐吾家四官见之，当为相思死 - If my four officials see her, they will die of love, a hyperbolic expression of admiration.
  • 引入小阁，云即四官香巢 - She led him into a small pavilion, saying it was the nest of the four officials.
  • 壁上诗笺，极奈莺燕，床头舄，奁畔粉，镜边钗，已见一斑 - Poems on the wall, very much like swallows, shoes by the bed, powder by the chest, and hairpins by the mirror, showing a glimpse of her elegance.
  • 少顷，阿二阿三来，貌俱纤宛，亦极奉承 - After a while, A'er and A'san came, both were delicate and graceful, and also very attentive.
  • 旋进珍馐，穷治水陆，笙歌鼎沸，各献所长 - Soon, fine dishes were served, a variety of seafood and freshwater delicacies, music and singing filled the air, and everyone showed their best.

  • 論郎表表 - Poetic or archaic expression meaning 'to commend the young man's excellence' or 'to praise the gentleman's outstanding qualities.'
  • 唯四官方稱 - 'Only the Fourth Official is worthy of praise' or 'Only the Fourth Official is suitable for this title,' with '四官' likely referring to a character or title.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 年余 - Indicates a period of more than a year, often used to denote an unspecified duration.
  • 三生一子一女，四无出 - Describes the family situation where three children (one son and two daughters) are born, but four others do not have offspring.
  • 二潜与生私 - Suggests that two individuals (likely referring to the wife and the concubine) have a secret relationship.
  • 亦生二子 - Indicates that this secret relationship also results in the birth of two sons.
  • 计迷于此者，三年余 - Means that someone (likely the narrator) has been confused or entangled in this situation for more than three years.
  • 偶归，不常见卓，均云归宁 - Describes the narrator occasionally returning home, rarely seeing his wife (卓), and hearing that she has gone back to her parents' house.
  • 计良得 - Means that the narrator considers himself to have made a good decision or arrangement.
  • 索资渐以钗钏，又以衣履且以书画玩具来变质 - Describes how the wife gradually demands jewelry, clothing, shoes, and even art and toys, leading to a deterioration in their relationship.
  • 索忽靳，因詈仆，仆蹀躞至再，携一册来 - The wife suddenly becomes stingy, curses the servant, and the servant returns twice with a book.
  • 娘子传语，家中产已罄，孑然一身，实不能作娼饱郎欲 - The wife's message through the servant indicates that the family's wealth is exhausted, and she is alone and unable to satisfy the narrator's desires.
  • 问田宅，曰：“货去久矣。” - When asked about the family's property, the servant replies that it has been sold long ago.
  • 生大惊，阅其册，细流支取年月，田宅售价，甚详 - The narrator is shocked and reads the book, which details the exact dates and prices of the property sales.
  • 宝山已空，日乞食于尼寺 - The wife's situation is described as empty, and she now lives in a temple and begs for food.
  • 急趋归，寻卓，则门户犹是，而主者已非 - The narrator rushes back home to find that the house is still the same, but the owner (卓) is no longer there.
  • 以妻卓卖券示 - The new owner shows the narrator a deed indicating that the wife (卓) has sold the house.
  • 问妻无耗，寻仆，忽不见 - The narrator asks about the wife and the servant, but neither can be found.
  • 无计，再返白家，则搬运一空，玉人早散 - With no other options, the narrator returns to the white family (likely a reference to a previous benefactor or place of residence), only to find everything moved out and the beautiful people (玉人) dispersed.
  • 居停遣仆洒扫，下逐客令 - The host sends the servant to clean and gives the narrator an eviction notice.
  • 茕茕顾影，托足无区，赧而求依于亲族，不许 - The narrator feels lonely and has nowhere to go, and when he seeks help from relatives, they refuse.
  • 无已，寄古寺宿 - With no other choice, the narrator stays in an old temple.
  • 久之，为乞于村郭，悬鹑百结，呼号两年 - After a long time, the narrator begins to beg in the village, looking destitute and calling out for help for two years.
  • 西风骤来，鸡皮皴裂 - The cold wind suddenly arrives, causing the narrator's skin to crack.
  • 欲觅死无法，意不若为梁上君子，得则苟且生，犯则杖下毙，犹胜于自戕 - The narrator considers suicide but finds no way, and instead decides that being a thief might be better, as it offers a chance to live or die by the law rather than taking his own life.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三生一子一女，四无出 - Reflects traditional family values and the importance of having male heirs.
  • 二潜与生私 - Suggests a breach of marital fidelity and the existence of concubines, which was common in certain historical periods.
  • 计迷于此者，三年余 - Indicates a prolonged period of confusion or entanglement, possibly due to complex family dynamics or financial issues.
  • 索资渐以钗钏，又以衣履且以书画玩具来变质 - Highlights the gradual deterioration of a relationship due to financial demands and materialism.
  • 问田宅，曰：“货去久矣。” - Reflects the importance of land and property in traditional Chinese society.
  • 宝山已空，日乞食于尼寺 - Suggests a fall from grace and the desperate situation of the wife.
  • 茕茕顾影，托足无区 - Describes the narrator's loneliness and lack of support.
  • 无已，寄古寺宿 - Shows the narrator's resort to a temple for shelter, reflecting the role of temples as places of refuge in traditional Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 卓 - The name of the narrator's wife.
  • 白家 - Likely refers to a previous benefactor or place of residence.
  • 玉人 - Refers to the beautiful people, likely a metaphor for the narrator's former associates or family members.

  • 阡陌更广 - Means 'the fields and paths are wider' or 'the fields and roads are broader.'

  • 目睹子成名，毕婚嫁 - Means 'to see one's sons become famous and to see them marry off' or 'to witness one's sons achieve fame and complete their marriages.'
  • 寿八十，犹抱孙 - Means 'to live to eighty and still have grandchildren' or 'to reach eighty and still have grandsons.'
  • 不敢出门户 - Means 'to dare not leave the home' or 'to not dare to go out of the house.'
  • 懊侬氏曰 - A phrase indicating that the author, 懊侬氏 (Ao Nong), is speaking.
  • 孟光举青玉案 - A reference to the story of Ban Jieyu, who used an incident involving a jade case to criticize her husband.
  • 文君作《白头吟》 - A reference to the story of Zhuo Wenjun, who wrote the poem 'White Hair Song' to express her dissatisfaction with her husband.
  • 卓哉二娘 - A phrase praising the Second Lady as outstanding.
  • 以不谏为谏 - Means 'to use not advising as a form of advice' or 'to use silence as a form of advice.'

  • 曰：“将若何？” - She asked, 'What should we do?', where '若何' means 'what should we do?'.

  • 岫审度久之，曰：“且同归，哀于主，或有主仆分。若作子夏之门人小子，则吾岂敢！” - Xiu pondered for a long time and said, 'Let's go together, weep before the master, and perhaps there will be a master-servant relationship. If I were to become a student of Zixia, a disciple of Confucius, I dare not!', where '子夏之门人小子' refers to a humble student of Zixia.
  • 落照坠崦嵫。 - The setting sun fell on Yanzhi, where '崦嵫' is a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with the sunset.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 項梁 - Xiang Liang, a prominent figure in the Chu-Han Contention, a period of civil war in the late Qin Dynasty.
  • 義帝 - Yi Di, a title given to Liu Ying, the son of Liu Bang (the founder of the Han Dynasty), who was later killed by Xiang Yu.
  • 盱眙 - Xuyi, a city in present-day Jiangsu Province, historically significant during the Chu-Han Contention.
  • 黔布 - Qian Bu, a general who killed Liu Ying (Yi Di).
  • 長公主 - Long Princess, a high-ranking princess in the Han Dynasty.
  • 子夏 - Zixia, a disciple of Confucius, known for his teachings on ritual and music.

  • 其中有《剑诀》一册，最合己意 - Among them, there was a volume of *Jiànjué* (Sword Manual), which best suited her own inclinations, indicating that the girl found particular interest in a specific text.
  • 潜自演习 - Secretly practiced it herself, suggesting that the girl engaged in private practice, possibly due to the sensitive nature of the content.
  • 岫知之，笑曰：“妹将学妙手空空儿乎？” - Xiù knew about it and laughed, saying, 'Are you going to learn the skills of the Empty-Handed Master?', a playful reference to a legendary figure known for his skillful and elusive abilities.
  • 曰：“然。” - She replied, 'Yes.', confirming the girl's intention.
  • 曰：“妹有幽恨，学此恐非所宜也。” - Xiù said, 'You have deep-seated grievances, and learning this might not be appropriate.', suggesting that Xiù is concerned about the potential consequences of the girl's actions.
  • 然无神人授以秘宝，学亦不成，徒搅清课耳。 - However, without a divine being to bestow secret treasures, learning this would be futile, only disrupting your studies, indicating that Xiù believes the girl needs divine assistance to succeed in her endeavors.
  • 一日，庭前木苓花开，灿烂如火 - One day, the *mùlíng* flowers in the courtyard bloomed, shining brightly like fire, describing a beautiful and vibrant scene.

  • 侍者各奏技，或管弦，或歌舞，或献书画 - The attendants performed various skills, some playing music and strings, others dancing and singing, and some presenting paintings and calligraphy, describing the diverse performances at the feast.
  • 岫则以手采都梁香草为寿 - Xiù picked *dūliáng* fragrant grass with her hands as a birthday gift, indicating a thoughtful and personal gesture.
  • 女时立阶下，主顾之，笑曰：“女学士何绝无所长耶？” - The girl stood at the bottom of the stairs, and the princess noticed her, smiling and saying, 'What are you, a scholar, without any special talents?', suggesting a playful and somewhat critical comment from the princess.
  • 女应命而起，聚壁上长剑之雌雄者，揎袖舞翩翩，飘风疾雨，寒光逼人 - The girl rose in response, gathered the male and female long swords on the wall, rolled up her sleeves, and danced gracefully, with the wind and rain swirling around, and the cold light shining brightly, describing a dramatic and impressive performance.

  • 婢子不才，愿为一往 - 'I am unworthy, but I am willing to go,' a modest yet determined statement.

  • 纫兰幼颖敏，貌丽性贞 - '幼颖敏' (young and sharp-witted), '貌丽' (beautiful appearance), and '性贞' (virtuous character) are descriptive phrases.

  • 母闻之，不怿曰：“是儿何语出不祥？” - '不怿' (displeased), '何语出不祥' (what unlucky words) describe the mother's reaction.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 今有人焉 - Classical formula meaning 'there is someone today' or 'there exists someone now,' introducing a hypothetical or real person.
  • 光天化日之中 - Classical phrase meaning 'under the bright sky and open day,' referring to a time and place where actions should be visible and just.
  • 九陌通衢之地 - Refers to a busy, well-traveled public square or thoroughfare, literally 'nine roads and open roads.'
  • 公然攫财物 - 'Openly seize property,' a strong phrase indicating brazen theft in public.
  • 使行道者耳如不闻，目如不见者 - Rhetorical construction meaning 'making those who pass by as if they did not hear or see,' emphasizing the shocking or ignored nature of the crime.
  • 谁欤？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'who is it?' used to draw attention to the identity of the thief.
  • 即北之剪绺，南之扒儿手也 - Parallel construction identifying the thieves by region and method: 'the northern pickpocket' and 'the southern pickpocket.'
  • 尤称巨擘 - 'Especially renowned as a master' or 'especially considered a leading figure.'
  • 其先癞母，本习猴婆技者 - 'His mother, originally a practitioner of the 'monkey woman's' art.' This refers to a traditional performance or skill, possibly a form of street performance or illusion.
  • 操此术游江湖间 - 'Practicing this art and traveling among the rivers and lakes,' a metaphor for wandering or traveling in the world.
  • 遇猴婆莫逆 - 'Met the monkey woman and became close friends,' using '莫逆' to mean deep friendship or kindred spirit.
  • 遂成夫妇 - 'Thus became husband and wife,' indicating a union formed through shared interests or skills.
  • 年十五六，其父母已饱食暖衣，称小康焉 - 'At fifteen or sixteen, his parents had already achieved a comfortable life, calling it 'xiaokang' (moderate prosperity).'
  • 出资送小癞子就村傅读 - 'Spent money to send Xiao Laizi to a village teacher to study,' indicating an attempt to provide an education.
  • 俾改业 - 'To have him change his profession,' suggesting a desire for the child to pursue a different path.
  • 癞性最敏，咿唔之暇，辄习弄好身手 - 'Xiao Laizi's nature was most agile; during his study sessions, he would practice and refine his physical skills,' showing his natural talent and skill in physical performance.
  • 恐坠箕裘 - 'Fear of falling short of the family tradition,' a classical phrase referring to not living up to one's ancestors' legacy.
  • 二老寿花甲 - 'The two elders celebrated their sixtieth birthday,' referring to a traditional milestone in Chinese culture.
  • 座客方称觞祝嘏 - 'The guests were just raising their cups to toast and offer blessings,' a common practice at celebratory occasions.
  • 癞子遽跪请仍习父业 - 'Xiao Laizi suddenly knelt and requested to resume his father's profession,' showing a decisive return to his family's trade.

  • 所谓如与可画竹，兔起鹘落，少纵即逝者也 - As in the case of Wuyu painting bamboo, where the brush moves like a hare and a hawk, and if you miss the moment, it’s gone, where '与可画竹' refers to a famous painter, and '兔起鹘落' describes the swift, precise movements of a painter.
  • 否则带水拖泥，动遭诟辱 - Otherwise, you’ll drag along with water and mud, and constantly face criticism, where '带水拖泥' is a metaphor for dragging along with unnecessary complications, and '动遭诟辱' means to constantly face criticism or disgrace.

  • 求称侄纳币 - Means 'to request to be called nephew and to pay a dowry'.
  • 不从，则称侄孙 - Means 'if not agreed upon, then to be called great-nephew'.
  • 直学士院高应松不肯草表 - Means 'the Director of the Imperial Academy Gao Yongsong refused to draft the petition'.
  • 改命京局官刘裒然为之 - Means 'changed the order to have the official Liu Pou Ran from the capital bureau draft it'.
  • 岳等至高邮稽家庄，为义民稽耸所杀 - Means 'Liu Yue and others arrived at Gao You Ji Family Village and were killed by the righteous man Ji Song'.
  • 吾邑北乡临大湖 - Means 'our county's northern village borders a large lake'.
  • 土人云：即宋之长天关，至今名为小关 - Means 'the locals say it was the Song Dynasty's Changtian Pass, which is still called Xiaoguan today'.
  • 城垣廨署，基址犹存 - Means 'the city walls and government offices still have their foundations'.
  • 隔湖三四里，宛在水中央 - Means 'separated by a lake, about three or four miles away, it seems to be in the middle of the water'.
  • 有生聚百家 - Means 'there are over a hundred households living there'.
  • 即稽家庄 - Means 'that is Ji Family Village'.
  • 众稽所居 - Means 'the residence of the Ji family'.
  • 耸之子孙也 - Means 'they are descendants of Ji Song'.
  • 足征宋时此处尚有陆路可通 - Means 'it is sufficient evidence that during the Song Dynasty, there was still a land route accessible here'.
  • 是必柳岳过江，由真至长，由长往邮，道出稽庄，始为耸杀耳 - Means 'it must be that Liu Yue crossed the river, went from Zhen to Chang, and then to Gao You, passing through Ji Village, where he was killed by Ji Song'.
  • 尤奇者 - Means 'especially strange is that'.
  • 前明枢密院罗万象 - Refers to Luo Wanxiang, a high-ranking official in the Ming Dynasty.
  • 清鲠不阿 - Means 'clear and upright, not fawning'.
  • 正笏垂绅有大度 - Means 'holding his笏 (a ceremonial tablet) upright and wearing a绅 (a sash), he had great dignity'.
  • 曾为天长宰 - Means 'he once served as the governor of Tianchang'.
  • 政绩甚夥 - Means 'he had many political achievements'.
  • 乡人爱之如慈母 - Means 'the villagers loved him like a mother'.
  • 鼎革后 - Refers to after the change of dynasty.
  • 隐于城门乡 - Means 'he retired to Chengmen Village'.
  • 自号湖滨旅舍 - Means 'he called himself Hu Bin Lushi (Lake Shore Traveler's Inn)'.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 恭宗 - Refers to Emperor Gong of Song, the last emperor of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 陈宜中 - A prominent official during the Southern Song Dynasty who served as a chancellor.
  • 柳岳 - A diplomat sent to negotiate with the Yuan army.
  • 陆秀夫 - A loyal minister of the Southern Song Dynasty who committed suicide with the last emperor after the fall of the dynasty.
  • 吕师孟 - Another loyal minister of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 囊加歹 - A Mongol envoy.
  • 文信国公 - A posthumous title given to Wen Tianxiang, a famous loyalist minister of the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 高应松 - The Director of the Imperial Academy during the Southern Song Dynasty.
  • 刘裒然 - An official who was ordered to draft the petition.
  • 稽耸 - A local hero who killed Liu Yue.
  • 长天关 - A strategic pass during the Song Dynasty, located in what is now Anhui Province.
  • 小关 - The modern name for the pass.
  • 罗万象 - A high-ranking official in the Ming Dynasty known for his integrity.
  • 天长 - A county in Anhui Province.
  • 鼎革 - Refers to the change of dynasty, specifically the fall of the Southern Song and the rise of the Yuan Dynasty.

  • 柳岳 - Liu Yue.
  • 陆秀夫 - Lu Xiufu.
  • 吕师孟 - Lü Shimeng.
  • 囊加歹 - Nang Jia Dai.
  • 文信国公 - Wen Xingguo Gong (posthumous title of Wen Tianxiang).
  • 高应松 - Gao Yongsong.
  • 刘裒然 - Liu Pou Ran.
  • 稽耸 - Ji Song.
  • 长天关 - Changtian Pass.
  • 小关 - Xiaoguan (modern name for the pass).
  • 罗万象 - Luo Wanxiang.
  • 天长 - Tianchang (county in Anhui Province).
  • 鼎革 - Change of dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 黄冠草履 - Describes a Taoist or hermit-like appearance, indicating a life of simplicity and detachment from worldly concerns.
  • 陇亩经行 - Suggests a life of farming and rural simplicity, reflecting the values of the time.
  • 田衣泥屐辈 - Refers to common farmers, indicating a humble social status.
  • 劝植农桑 - Reflects the importance of agriculture and sericulture in Chinese society.
  • 教义学子弟 - Indicates the value placed on education and moral instruction.
  • 梦一青衣来招 - Suggests a supernatural or divine intervention, common in Chinese folklore.
  • 奉迓玉趾 - A polite expression used to show respect, often used in formal or ceremonial contexts.
  • 揖而就坐 - A formal greeting and seating arrangement, indicating a respectful and formal setting.
  • 视其貌，如王者 - Describes a regal appearance, suggesting a high-ranking or divine figure.
  • 绿袍珠履 - Describes a high-ranking or divine figure, often associated with deities or spirits.
  • 须髯飘飘 - Suggests a dignified and powerful appearance, often associated with deities or wise men.
  • 血食于此 - Indicates a deity or spirit, often associated with temples and shrines.
  • 庙中签诗俚俗鄙恶 - Criticism of the temple's divination poems, suggesting a need for improvement.
  • 敬知先生一代才人 - Shows respect and recognition of the person's literary talent.
  • 衙官屈宋 - Refers to ancient poets Qu Yuan and Song Yu, suggesting a high level of literary talent.
  • 敢乞赐以珠玉 - Requests the person to write something valuable, indicating a high regard for the person's literary skills.
  • 冥冥增辉 - Suggests the importance of the request, indicating that the writing will have a lasting impact.
  • 载拜退 - A formal farewell, indicating respect and formality.
  • 逾阈惊寤 - Suggests a sudden realization or confusion, indicating a state of shock.
  • 不甚信 - Indicates skepticism, suggesting a lack of belief in the dream.
  • 又入梦 - Indicates a recurring dream, suggesting a persistent message or theme.
  • 诿尤谆切 - Shows a strong and persistent request, indicating the urgency and importance of the matter.
  • 异之 - Indicates surprise or disbelief, suggesting a sense of wonder.
  • 关之城隍庙 - Refers to the city god temple in the city of Guan, a place of worship and spiritual significance.
  • 庭阶曲折 - Describes the layout of the temple, suggesting a mystical or sacred atmosphere.
  • 依稀梦游 - Suggests a sense of unreality, indicating a dream-like state.
  • 鄙人鲁拙机滞 - Indicates self-deprecation, suggesting a lack of confidence or ability.
  • 忧患膺心 - Suggests personal difficulties, indicating a troubled past.
  • 乱离亡命 - Indicates a turbulent and dangerous period, suggesting a history of hardship.
  • 风雅事久已抛却 - Suggests a loss of interest in literary pursuits, indicating a shift in priorities.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然既承隆委，敢自讳其拙耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Having already accepted such a significant responsibility, how dare I hide my own inadequacies?' It reflects humility and self-awareness.
  • 容即拟稿求教 - Polite phrase meaning 'I will draft a response and seek your guidance,' indicating a respectful request for advice.
  • 色犹怒 - Description of a person's angry expression.
  • 旁有冠带客，修髯面白晰，若幕宾状 - Detailed description of a guest: 'a bearded man with a white, clear complexion, dressed like a retainer or guest.' This suggests a dignified or scholarly appearance.
  • 代公缓颊甚殷 - Phrase meaning 'spoke on your behalf very earnestly,' indicating someone advocating for you with great sincerity.
  • 色始霁 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the expression softened,' implying a change from anger to calm.
  • 即命送公出 - Command meaning 'ordered to escort you out,' indicating a formal dismissal.
  • 拱而别 - Formal gesture of farewell, meaning 'bowed and parted.'
  • 问王者何人 - Question meaning 'asked who the king was,' showing curiosity about the identity of the ruler.
  • 噤声 - Command meaning 'be silent,' indicating a request for secrecy.
  • 以指划公腕，书曰 - Gesture meaning 'wrote on your wrist with a finger,' indicating a secret or symbolic message.
  • 盥沐作诗三十首 - Description of an action meaning 'washed and composed thirty poems,' indicating a ritual or creative act.
  • 清丽缠绵，温柔敦厚 - Descriptive phrases meaning 'clear and beautiful, lingering and tender, gentle and sincere,' used to describe the style of the poems.
  • 缮稿焚之神前，而后书木悬之壁 - Sequence of actions meaning 'prepared the draft, burned it before the deity, and then wrote it on wood and hung it on the wall,' indicating a ritual of offering and preservation.
  • 自叙甚详 - Phrase meaning 'spoke in great detail about himself,' indicating a thorough account.
  • 文山先生与其客杜浒等亡入真州 - Narrative meaning 'Mr. Wen Shan and his guest Du Hu escaped to Zhenzhou,' indicating a historical event.
  • 苗再成绐之出 - Phrase meaning 'Miao Zai Cheng deceived them into going out,' indicating a betrayal or trick.
  • 制司下令，捕文丞相 - Phrase meaning 'the provincial command issued an order to capture Chancellor Wen,' indicating a legal or political action.
  • 素闻稽名，乃反驭访稽 - Sequence of actions meaning 'having heard of Ji's reputation, he went to visit Ji,' indicating a pursuit of knowledge or guidance.
  • 稽迎之于家，事之甚谨 - Description of actions meaning 'Ji welcomed him at home and treated him with great respect,' indicating hospitality.
  • 慷慨泣下 - Phrase meaning 'cried emotionally,' indicating a display of deep feeling.
  • 散家资，招健丁 - Sequence of actions meaning 'dispersed his family wealth and recruited strong men,' indicating a mobilization of resources.
  • 遣其子与客伴送文山先生至泰州，航海达闽 - Narrative meaning 'sent his son and guests to accompany Mr. Wen Shan to Taizhou, where they sailed to Fujian,' indicating a journey or mission.
  • 生前道经于此，殁即为此乡之神 - Phrase meaning 'before his death he passed through here, and after his death he became the deity of this village,' indicating a transformation into a local spirit.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 文山 - Refers to Wen Tianxiang (1236–1283), a famous Southern Song dynasty loyalist minister and poet who resisted the Mongol invasion.
  • 杜浒 - A historical figure who accompanied Wen Tianxiang during his escape.
  • 苗再成 - A historical figure who betrayed Wen Tianxiang.
  • 制司 - Refers to the provincial command or government authority.
  • 文丞相 - A title for Wen Tianxiang, emphasizing his role as a high-ranking official.
  • 稽名 - Refers to the reputation or fame of Ji, a historical figure.
  • 泰州 - A place name in Jiangsu Province.
  • 闽 - A place name referring to Fujian Province.

Proper Nouns

  • 文山 - Wen Tianxiang.
  • 杜浒 - Du Hu.
  • 苗再成 - Miao Zai Cheng.
  • 制司 - Provincial command or government authority.
  • 文丞相 - Chancellor Wen Tianxiang.
  • 稽名 - The reputation or fame of Ji.
  • 泰州 - Taizhou.
  • 闽 - Fujian.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 至稽公殁为先生幕中客 - Upon Jigong's death, became a guest in the master's entourage.
  • 岂非订生死交欤 - Rhetorical question meaning 'isn't this a seal of a life-and-death friendship?'
  • 前曾游庙堂 - Previously served in the imperial court.
  • 悚然瞻仰 - With awe, to look up to or admire.
  • 中殿肖信国公像 - The image of Xin Guo Gong in the main hall.
  • 少偏肖儒衣冠者 - A figure dressed in the attire of a scholar standing slightly to the side.
  • 正襟危坐 - Classical phrase meaning 'to sit upright and solemnly.'
  • 英风侠骨 - Heroic spirit and chivalrous bones.
  • 凛凛如生 - Majestic and awe-inspiring, as if alive.
  • 闻之土人云 - Heard from the locals say.
  • 罗公卒于是乡 - Lu Gong died in this village.
  • 葬长亭 - Buried in a long pavilion or a place where travelers rest.
  • 四围多孤冢 - Surrounded by many isolated graves.
  • 泛泛若水上凫 - Floating like ducks on water.
  • 余时拟出资为公加封植 - Planned to contribute money to improve the tomb.
  • 立崇碑 - To erect a grand stele.
  • 叙其事 - To narrate the story.
  • 肖像于信国右 - To place a portrait to the right of Xin Guo Gong.
  • 宛与稽公对峙 - As if facing each other.
  • 血食千秋 - To be worshipped with sacrifices for thousands of years.
  • 惜阮囊依旧 - Regret that the small purse remains the same.
  • 奈何 - What can be done?
  • 懊侬氏曰 - A literary name, said.
  • 由宋末至明末 - From the end of the Song dynasty to the end of the Ming dynasty.
  • 相隔垂四百余年 - Separated by almost four hundred years.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 稽公 - Refers to Jigong, a historical figure.
  • 先生 - Refers to a respected teacher or mentor.
  • 信国公 - Refers to Xin Guo Gong, a historical figure, likely a high-ranking official or noble.
  • 罗公 - Refers to Lu Gong, another historical figure.

  • 中殿 - Refers to the main hall of a temple or a significant building.
  • 长亭 - Refers to a long pavilion or a place where travelers rest, often used in classical literature to symbolize a place of farewell or reflection.
  • 孤冢 - Refers to isolated graves, often used in classical literature to evoke a sense of loneliness or desolation.
  • 阮囊 - Refers to a small purse, often used in classical literature to symbolize limited financial resources.

  • 稽公 - Jigong, a historical figure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生为河岳，殁为日星 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'born as a river or mountain, deceased as the sun or stars,' used to describe a person of great significance.
  • 拳拳于弹丸小邑 - Phrase meaning 'firmly attached to a small town,' used to describe someone's deep affection or commitment to a place.
  • 亡命流离 - Phrase meaning 'fleeing in exile,' used to describe someone who is on the run or in a state of displacement.
  • 襟怀朗然 - Phrase meaning 'broad and clear-minded,' used to describe someone with a clear and open mind.
  • 定静若此 - Phrase meaning 'so calm and composed,' used to describe someone's state of mind.
  • 声气相通 - Phrase meaning 'spirits and breaths are connected,' used to describe a deep spiritual or emotional connection.
  • 鼎足而不愧乎神 - Phrase meaning 'standing on equal footing without shame to the gods,' used to describe someone's noble and honorable status.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 文公 - Refers to a historical figure, possibly a ruler or a notable person, whose name is not specified in the text.
  • 稽庄 - Refers to a specific place, possibly a village or a town, where the events described took place.
  • 罗公 - Refers to another historical figure, possibly a noble or a notable person, whose name is not specified in the text.

  • 野老 - Refers to a reclusive or hermit-like person, often associated with a simple and humble lifestyle.
  • 忠魂 - Refers to a loyal spirit, often associated with a person who has died with honor and loyalty.
  • 汊涧大觉寺 - Refers to a specific temple, possibly a well-known or historically significant place of worship.
  • 住持僧 - Refers to the head monk or abbot of a temple, often responsible for the spiritual and administrative leadership of the temple.

Proper Nouns

  • 三君子 - Term meaning 'three gentlemen,' used to refer to three distinguished individuals.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 丐癖 - Character trait or condition, possibly referring to a fondness for beggars or a peculiar behavior.
  • 補博士弟子員 - To be admitted as a student of the Imperial Academy, indicating a form of education.
  • 戚里推為國器 - Recommended by relatives and friends as a national talent, suggesting high regard and potential.
  • 笑却之 - Laughed at and rejected, indicating a dismissive reaction.
  • 我命中雖無十二钗 - Reference to the Twelve Beauties of Jinling from *Dream of the Red Chamber*, implying a lack of romantic or marital prospects.
  • 家赤貧 - Extremely poor, indicating severe financial hardship.
  • 日把卷，不顾灶突尘 - Daily holding books, disregarding the smoke from the stove, suggesting intense dedication to study despite poverty.
  • 同人偶拉往东廓观戏 - Fellow scholars occasionally took him to watch a play in Dongguo, indicating a social activity.
  • 一二阙，即称赏 - After a few acts, he praised it, showing his appreciation for the performance.
  • 演荥阳公子教歌 - Refers to a specific play or performance about a nobleman teaching songs.
  • 鳏寡孤独之流，疲癃残疾之辈 - Refers to the marginalized and disadvantaged, including widowers, widows, orphans, the elderly, and the disabled.
  • 妙哉！此天人菩萨，宰官身而说法像也 - Wonderful! This is like a heavenly being or a bodhisattva, a high official speaking like a sage, expressing admiration for the performance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 十二钗 - The Twelve Beauties of Jinling from *Dream of the Red Chamber*, a famous novel by Cao Xueqin.
  • 博士弟子员 - Students of the Imperial Academy, a prestigious institution in ancient China.
  • 东廓 - Likely refers to a specific location or area where plays were performed.
  • 荥阳公子 - Refers to a nobleman from the city of Xingyang, known for his cultured and refined tastes.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 人以為颠 - Literally 'people think it is crazy,' but can be interpreted as 'people consider it foolish' or 'people regard it as madness.'
  • 不识不知，无拘无管 - Describes a state of being free from knowledge and constraints, living in a natural and unencumbered way.
  • 以天地为蘧庐 - Metaphorically means 'to regard the world as a temporary dwelling,' suggesting a transient and carefree existence.
  • 以日月为灯烛 - Metaphorically means 'to regard the sun and moon as lamps,' implying a life in harmony with nature.
  • 以江河为襟带 - Metaphorically means 'to regard rivers as belts,' suggesting a life that is expansive and free.
  • 以木石为友朋 - Metaphorically means 'to regard trees and stones as friends,' indicating a life that is simple and unpretentious.
  • 歌哭无常，叫号随己 - Describes a life of spontaneous and unrestrained emotions, where one sings and cries as they please.
  • 是真薄诸侯而不为，比散仙而无愧者 - Means 'this is truly someone who despises the nobility and does not seek their status, and who compares himself to a wandering immortal without shame.'
  • 何乐如之 - Means 'what greater joy could there be?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 丐也者 - Refers to a beggar or someone who lives a simple, unburdened life.
  • 鄧攸戚 - Refers to a son who is like the virtuous son of the historical figure Deng You.
  • 中秋 - Refers to the traditional Chinese Mid-Autumn Festival.
  • 寺人 - Refers to a eunuch, who has no right to keep the entire estate.
  • 宗祧 - Refers to the family lineage and the importance of continuing it.

Proper Nouns

  • 生 - The name of the character who speaks the quoted statement.
  • 鄧攸 - A historical figure known for his virtue and the story of his son, who is referred to as '鄧攸戚' (Deng You's son).
  • 宰 - The magistrate who intervenes in the family's affairs.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嫠叩首 - A widow bows her head, indicating deep respect or plea.
  • 族子皆豚犬耳 - A metaphor suggesting that the family members are like pigs and dogs, implying they are of little value or worth.
  • 与其遗风木忧 - A phrase indicating that it is better not to leave behind an empty sorrow or regret.
  • 盍若视亡者成伯道 - A suggestion to treat the deceased as if they had achieved the status of a noble or respected figure.
  • 乞贤宰官出示于族人约 - A request to the wise and virtuous official to show the clan the agreement.
  • 凡子侄行 - Refers to the actions of all descendants.
  • 无论遐迩 - Regardless of distance or nearness, indicating inclusivity.
  • 秋试一获解 - To pass the imperial examination once.
  • 未亡人宁乞不怨也 - The widow would rather beg than complain.
  • 善 - Good, indicating approval.
  • 即示悬里门并花氏宗塾 - Immediately display it at the village gate and the Hua family temple school.
  • 族咸惶惶作希冀想 - The clan members all anxiously hope for something.
  • 临时掘井 - Digging a well at the last moment, indicating a rush or delay.
  • 茂才花根石 - A talented person named Hua Genshi.
  • 富不仁 - Wealthy but lacking in benevolence.
  • 夙涎嫠产者 - Someone who has long coveted the widow's property.
  • 浼生作捉刀人 - Asking someone to act as a proxy or agent.
  • 隽即酬千金 - If successful, a reward of a thousand gold pieces.
  • 生正苦囊涩 - The student is in dire financial straits.
  • 即乘便之钱塘 - Immediately take the opportunity to go to Qiantang.
  • 同舍馆 - Stay in the same lodging.
  • 石时出宿勾栏 - Hua Genshi often stays at a brothel.
  • 贸贸然来 - Comes uninvited or unexpectedly.
  • 君茂才花根石耶 - Are you the talented person Hua Genshi?
  • 姑应之 - Temporarily agree or respond.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 嫠 - A widow, often used in classical texts to denote a woman who has lost her husband.
  • 族子 - Family members, specifically descendants.
  • 豚犬 - Pigs and dogs, used metaphorically to indicate low value or worth.
  • 遗风木忧 - Leaving behind an empty sorrow or regret, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.
  • 成伯道 - Achieving the status of a noble or respected figure, often used in historical contexts.
  • 贤宰官 - A wise and virtuous official, often a local magistrate or administrator.
  • 秋试 - The imperial examination, a crucial event in the lives of scholars and officials.
  • 未亡人 - A widow, a term used to express the widow's status and feelings.
  • 钱塘 - Qiantang, a city in Zhejiang Province, known for its historical significance and natural beauty.
  • 勾栏 - A brothel or place of entertainment, often associated with the lower classes.

Proper Nouns

  • 嫠 - Widow.
  • 族子 - Family members.
  • 贤宰官 - Wise and virtuous official.
  • 茂才花根石 - Hua Genshi, a talented person.
  • 钱塘 - Qiantang, a city in Zhejiang Province.
  • 勾栏 - Brothel or place of entertainment.

[320]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 曷試言長上名字事迹 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Why not try stating the names, deeds, and background of the superior officials?' Used to prompt an explanation or revelation.
  • 袖出一函與之曰 - Phrase describing a person taking a letter from their sleeve and giving it to someone, a common gesture in classical literature to denote a secret or important message.
  • 此家主所持贈者，秘莫示人 - Phrase indicating that the letter is a gift from the household head and is meant to be kept secret and not shown to others.
  • 揭晓来讨喜觞饮耳 - Euphemistic way of saying the letter's contents are to be revealed for the purpose of a celebratory gathering.
  • 生潜阅之，闱中关节也 - 'The student secretly read it and found it to be a ‘joint’ in the examination hall' – '关节' (jìoàn) refers to a secret connection or bribe used in imperial examinations.
  • 盖石父亦明经，有富名 - 'For the father [Stone] is also a scholar of the classics and has a reputation for wealth' – '明经' refers to a degree in the Confucian classics, and '有富名' indicates he is known for being wealthy.
  • 帘官某与有素，媚之希丰酬 - 'A certain official in charge of the examination hall had a longstanding relationship with him, and flattered him in hopes of a generous reward' – '帘官' refers to an official in charge of the examination hall, and '希丰酬' means to hope for a generous return.
  • 适来者，价也 - 'The one who came was the price' – a metaphorical way of saying that the person who came was the bribe or the intermediary.
  • 生怀入闱，草草与石文 - 'The student carried it into the examination hall and hastily wrote the essay for Stone' – '与石文' refers to writing the essay for Stone, indicating he used the bribe to pass the exam.
  • 自则略经营，嵌关节 - 'He himself only slightly worked on it, inserting the connection' – '经营' here refers to careful planning or preparation, and '嵌关节' means to insert the secret connection or bribe into the exam.
  • 及榜发，石落而生魁矣 - 'When the results were posted, Stone failed while the student came first' – '魁' is the top rank in the examination results.
  • 荣归在途，方自惭蓬荜湫隘 - 'While returning in glory, he began to feel ashamed of his humble dwelling' – '蓬荜湫隘' is a humble expression for one's own home, literally 'humble thatch and narrow rooms.'
  • 比入里门，而四嫠已拥之至家 - 'By the time he entered the village gate, four widows had already gathered him to his home' – '里门' is the village gate or neighborhood entrance, and '四嫠' refers to four widows.
  • 满堂设红氍毹，璧上泥光可耀目 - 'The hall was filled with red felt carpets, and the walls gleamed with painted light' – '红氍毹' is a red felt carpet, often used in ceremonial or celebratory settings.
  • 会宰亦呵道来贺，生错愕不知所云 - 'The county magistrate also came to congratulate him, shouting orders for the way, and the student was stunned and did not know what to say' – '呵道' is a gesture of a high official ordering the way to be cleared, showing his status.
  • 嫠出叩谢玉成，生则再三辞 - 'The widows came out to thank him for his kindness, but the student repeatedly declined' – '玉成' is a respectful term for helping someone succeed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 明经 - A degree in the Confucian classics, one of the official examination subjects in the imperial examination system.
  • 帘官 - Officials responsible for the administration of the imperial examination, often involved in the distribution of exam questions and the prevention of cheating.
  • 闱中关节 - A secret connection or bribe used in the examination hall, often involving an intermediary or a bribe to secure a favorable result.
  • 闱 - The examination hall or examination chamber, a secluded area where candidates took the imperial exams.
  • 榜发 - The posting of the examination results, a major event in the imperial examination system.
  • 魁 - The top rank in the examination results, often called the 'first place' or 'top scholar.'
  • 里门 - The entrance to a village or neighborhood, used here to indicate the student's return to his home village.
  • 四嫠 - Four widows, a common literary trope in classical Chinese fiction, often representing poor or vulnerable women.
  • 红氍毹 - A red felt carpet, often used in ceremonial or celebratory contexts, symbolizing wealth and status.
  • 会宰 - The county magistrate or local official, a high-ranking figure in the local administration.
  • 呵道 - A gesture used by high officials to clear the way for their passage, showing their authority.
  • 玉成 - A respectful term for helping someone succeed, often used in the context of marriage or career advancement.

Proper Nouns

  • 石父 - A proper name, likely the father of the character 'Shi' (石), or a person with the surname 'Shi' and the honorific 'Fu' (father).
  • 生 - A common term for a scholar or student, often used in classical fiction to refer to the protagonist.
  • 石文 - Likely the name of an essay or the work of the character 'Shi' (石), or the name of the person for whom the essay was written.
  • 会宰 - A title or honorific for the county magistrate or local official.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宰曰 - The magistrate said, where '宰' is a title for a local official.
  • 有议约 - There is an agreement, referring to a formal arrangement or pact.
  • 何必辞 - Why bother to refuse? or Why need to decline? A rhetorical question implying acceptance.
  • 冠服 - Wearing ceremonial attire, indicating the formalization of a new status.
  • 叩四母与亡者木主 - Kneel before the four mothers and the spirit tablet of the deceased, a ritual act showing respect and filial piety.
  • 礼成始去 - The ceremony completed, he then left, indicating the conclusion of a formal ritual.
  • 得汝为子 - To have you as a son, expressing the joy of adoption.
  • 足为孀孤吐气 - Sufficient to give the widowed and orphaned a sense of relief, indicating the emotional impact on the family.
  • 家中不少阿堵物 - The home is not lacking in wealth, where '阿堵物' is an archaic term for money or wealth.
  • 听挥霍，不吝也 - Feel free to spend, without any stinginess, indicating the adoptive mother's generosity.
  • 诸母有子 - The mothers already have a son, indicating the existing family structure.
  • 鲸吞 - To swallow like a whale, a metaphor for excessive consumption.
  • 欲娶两妇 - Wants to marry two wives, indicating the desire to have multiple spouses.
  • 本生洁苹蘩 - To serve the biological parents with purity and diligence, where '苹蘩' refers to the duties of a daughter-in-law.
  • 诸慈奉榛栗 - To serve the adoptive parents with respect and diligence, where '榛栗' refers to the duties of a son.
  • 孀孤 - Widowed and orphaned, referring to the adoptive family members.
  • 昕夕定省如所生 - Morning and evening visits as if they were his biological parents, indicating the filial piety expected of the adopted son.
  • 钟爱如己出 - Treating him with love as if he were their own child, indicating the adoptive parents' affection.
  • 一裘一葛 - One fur coat and one cotton robe, referring to seasonal clothing.
  • 制必四 - Each item is made four times, indicating the consistency in quantity and quality.
  • 恐其判低昂也 - To ensure they are not of different quality, indicating the concern for uniformity.
  • 以来时敝衣私扃一箧 - He secretly locked away the old clothes he had brought, indicating the preservation of his past.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宰 - Refers to a local official, such as a magistrate or county head.
  • 冠服 - Ceremonial attire worn during important rituals, symbolizing a change in status.
  • 叩四母与亡者木主 - A traditional Chinese ritual where one kneels before the spirit tablets of ancestors and living elders to show respect.
  • 礼成始去 - Indicates the completion of a formal ritual, which is a significant moment in Chinese culture.
  • 阿堵物 - An archaic term for money or wealth, often used in a dismissive or informal way.
  • 鲸吞 - A metaphor for excessive consumption, often used to criticize greed.
  • 本生 - Refers to the biological parents.
  • 诸慈 - Refers to the adoptive parents.
  • 昕夕定省 - A practice of visiting parents in the morning and evening, showing filial piety.
  • 钟爱 - Deep affection, often used to describe parental love.

Proper Nouns

  • 阿堵物 - Archaic term for money or wealth.
  • 鲸吞 - Metaphor for excessive consumption.
  • 本生 - Biological parents.
  • 诸慈 - Adoptive parents.
  • 昕夕定省 - Practice of visiting parents in the morning and evening.
  • 钟爱 - Deep affection.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一朝 - A time, often used to indicate a specific moment or occasion.
  • 戚属 - Relatives, particularly those closely related.
  • 坐厅事 - Sitting in the hall, often used to indicate a formal setting.
  • 大声呼生名 - Shouting out the name of the person, indicating a loud and urgent call.
  • 嫠争出屏后 - The widow (嫠) steps out from behind the screen (屏), indicating a sudden and unexpected appearance.
  • 狗彘奴 - A derogatory term for a person, often used to express strong disapproval.
  • 尚敢尔耶 - Still dare to do this, indicating a challenge or rebuke.
  • 我辈有孝廉儿 - We have a filial and upright son (孝廉), indicating a claim of moral superiority.
  • 寺人产俱在 - The eunuchs and offspring are all present, indicating a reference to the family's status and connections.
  • 曷再攫乎 - Why seize again, indicating a demand to stop further action.
  • 呼仆缚之公庭 - Calling the servants to bind him in the public court, indicating a formal and public act of punishment.
  • 敲断狗胫股 - Breaking the dog's leg, indicating a violent act.
  • 诸仆应，声若雷 - The servants respond, their voices like thunder, indicating a powerful and unified reaction.
  • 怒目，臂露筋 - With angry eyes and exposed veins on their arms, indicating a display of anger and strength.
  • 执索将系维 - Holding ropes to tie him up, indicating the preparation for binding.
  • 戚惶急摇手曰 - The relative is in a state of panic and waves his hand, indicating a desperate attempt to stop the situation.
  • 何敢尔，何敢尔 - How dare you, how dare you, indicating repeated emphasis on the severity of the action.
  • 我辈来与贤郎作冰耳 - We came to make an ice (冰) with your noble son, indicating a formal proposal or match-making.
  • 姑毕其说 - Let me finish my explanation, indicating a request for patience.
  • 里有富室刘叟 - There is a wealthy man named Liu in the village, indicating a reference to a specific individual.
  • 老无子，唯五女 - He is old and has no sons, only five daughters, indicating a family situation.
  • 皆诸妾所生，年相若 - All the daughters are from concubines and are of similar age, indicating a specific family structure.
  • 极友爱 - Extremely loving, indicating a strong bond among the sisters.
  • 尝闺中设誓 - Once they made a vow in their chambers, indicating a private and solemn promise.
  • 愿同事一良人 - Wishing to share one good man, indicating a shared desire for a common husband.
  • 顷闻贤郎，力能享五美 - Recently heard that your noble son is capable of enjoying five beauties, indicating a claim of the son's desirability.
  • 故倩某等来执柯 - Therefore, we asked some to come to act as matchmakers, indicating a formal proposal.
  • 嫠始唯唯，改容逊谢 - The widow first nods, then changes her expression and humbly thanks, indicating a change in attitude.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 孝廉 - A title given to scholars who passed the imperial examination, indicating a high moral and academic standing.
  • 寺人 - Eunuchs, indicating a reference to the imperial court or a high-status family.
  • 冰 - A term used in ancient China to refer to a matchmaker or a formal proposal, indicating a cultural practice.

  • 诸妾 - Concubines, indicating a specific family structure and social hierarchy.
  • 闺中设誓 - Making a vow in the inner chambers, indicating a private and solemn promise among women.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 婚議成 - Indicates that the marriage has been arranged.
  • 婉倩里之素有德望者代月老 - The respected and virtuous person from Wanqian Village acted as the matchmaker. '月老' (Yuelao) is the Chinese God of Marriage.
  • 唯酬以酒肉而已 - Only rewarded with wine and meat, indicating a modest reward.
  • 亲迎之日 - On the day of the wedding, referring to the traditional Chinese wedding ceremony where the groom goes to the bride's home to fetch her.
  • 陪奁极丰 - The dowry was extremely rich, indicating a generous dowry.
  • 彩舆一队 - A line of decorated carriages, referring to the procession of carriages.
  • 如凤集，如鸦衔，如鱼贯 - Like phoenixes gathering, like crows carrying, like fish swimming in a line, describing the grand and orderly procession.
  • 固已奇矣 - Indeed, it was already strange, indicating that the scene was unusual.
  • 嫠更招优演剧 - The widow also hired actors to perform plays, indicating additional entertainment.
  • 生且请于是日设厂赈流人 - The groom also requested on that day to set up a relief station for displaced people, indicating a charitable act.
  • 鼓乐丝竹，与乞食诵佛声相错杂 - Drum music, string instruments, and the sounds of beggars reciting Buddhist scriptures were mixed together, describing the chaotic yet festive atmosphere.
  • 却扇后 - After removing the fan, a traditional gesture in Chinese weddings where the bride removes her fan to reveal her face.
  • 视五女，皆美绝伦 - Seeing the five daughters, all were exceptionally beautiful, indicating the beauty of the women.
  • 燕瘦环肥 - Like Yan's slender figure and Huán's plump figure, a reference to two famous beauties in Chinese history.
  • 各极态度 - Each showing their unique charm, indicating the individual beauty of each woman.
  • 香温玉暖，互斗尹邢 - Warm and fragrant, like jade, they competed with each other like Yin and Yang, a metaphor for the beauty and competition among the women.
  • 孟曰璧月，仲曰香月，叔曰好月，季曰琼月，五曰今月 - The eldest is named Bixue, the second Xiangyue, the third Hao Yue, the fourth Qiongyue, and the fifth Jinyue, indicating the names of the five daughters.
  • 姐妹善事姑与良人 - The sisters were good at serving their mother-in-law and husband, indicating their respectful and dutiful behavior.
  • 年余毫无争夕事 - For over a year, there was no dispute over night visits, indicating harmony in the household.
  • 生自嗣四嫠也 - The groom inherited four widows, indicating that the groom married a widow with three other wives.
  • 手不拈斑管，目不睹制艺，足不踏塾门 - He did not pick up a brush, did not see the examination essays, and did not step into the school, indicating his disinterest in scholarly pursuits.
  • 唯豪饮鼓哀弦，挈同好串繁响 - He only drank heavily, played sad music, and gathered friends to make loud noises, indicating his leisurely and bohemian lifestyle.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 婚议成 - Refers to the traditional Chinese custom of arranging marriages.
  • 月老 - The Chinese God of Marriage, often depicted as an old man tying couples' hands together with a red thread.
  • 亲迎之日 - Refers to the traditional Chinese wedding ceremony where the groom goes to the bride's home to fetch her.
  • 陪奁 - The dowry given by the bride's family to the groom's family.
  • 彩舆 - Decorated carriages used in traditional Chinese weddings.
  • 燕瘦环肥 - References to two famous beauties in Chinese history, Yan Zhenqing and Huán Yàn.
  • 香温玉暖 - Metaphors for beauty and warmth, often used to describe women.
  • 尹邢 - A metaphor for beauty and competition, referring to Yin and Yang, the complementary forces in Chinese philosophy.
  • 嗣四嫠 - Refers to the practice of polygamy in traditional Chinese society, where a man could inherit multiple widows.

Proper Nouns

  • 婉倩里 - The name of the village where the matchmaker lived.
  • 月老 - The Chinese God of Marriage.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 呀呀学语 - Common expression for young children beginning to speak.
  • 生卜于五相祠 - He sought divination at the Five-Phase Shrine.
  • 儿所以能生子者，父之荫，母之福，非儿所有也 - The reason I can have children is due to my father's protection and my mother's blessing, not because of me. This is a humble and grateful statement.
  • 诸母他日各抱孙，各教妇，胜于日对不才子 - One day, each of you will hold your own grandchild, teach your own daughter-in-law, which is better than daily dealing with an unworthy son. This is a comparison between the joy of grandchildren and the burden of an unworthy son.
