Idiomatic Expressions

  • 众诧其妄 - The crowd was surprised by his absurdity. Combines surprise (诧) with the idea of something being unreasonable or absurd (妄).
  • 索逋而来，逋偿而去 - He came seeking debt and left after paying it off. Metaphorical expression indicating fulfillment of purpose.
  • 潜更来时敝衣 - He secretly changed into the tattered clothes he had worn before. Suggests a change in appearance, possibly to blend in or disguise himself.
  • 飘然远引 - He departed lightly and far away. Conveys a sense of ease and distance in leaving.
  • 五子也 - He has five sons. Straightforward statement about the number of children.
  • 孟曰环，仲曰琅，叔曰琥，季曰珊，五曰玖 - His eldest son is named Ring, the second is named Ling, the third is named Amber, the fourth is named Coral, and the fifth is named Jade. Lists the names of the sons in order of birth.
  • 皆颖敏，早入泮 - All of them are sharp-witted and entered school early. Indicates that the sons are intelligent and started their education at a young age.
  • 琅补邑明经 - Ling passed the local examination for Confucian scholars. Refers to a specific academic achievement.
  • 环珊均乡魁 - Ring and Coral both won the top honors in their district. Indicates that they excelled in local competitions.
  • 琥中明通榜进士 - Amber passed the imperial examination and became a jinshi. Refers to a high-ranking academic success.
  • 玖食廪饩 - Jade receives a stipend from the government. Indicates that he is supported by the state.
  • 太母四，均长斋绣佛 - The four grandmothers all maintain a vegetarian diet and embroider Buddha statues. Describes their religious practices.
  • 委家政于妇 - They entrust household affairs to their wives. Indicates that the grandmothers leave domestic responsibilities to their wives.
  • 大太母寿八十五 - The eldest grandmother is eighty-five years old. Provides an age for the grandmother.
  • 孙环视于庭，嫠泫然 - Grandson Ring looked at her in the courtyard, and the widow wept. Describes an emotional scene.
  • 汝皆成名，亦知尚有父乎 - You have all become famous, but do you know there is still a father? Poignant question about the father's fate.
  • 乃涕不可抑，跪求祖母训 - She then could not stop crying and knelt to ask the grandmother for guidance. Describes an emotional and respectful act.
  • 昨梦菩萨指示 - Last night in a dream, the Bodhisattva gave instructions. Introduces a supernatural element.
  • 云汝父乞食于潇湘岸上，当往晤一面，了父子缘 - It said that your father is begging on the banks of the Xiang River, and you should go to meet him once to resolve the father-son bond. Provides a specific location and purpose for the journey.
  • 至湘水，凡有城廓村堡，无不暗物色 - Upon reaching the Xiang River, they searched every city, village, and fort, trying to find him. Describes the effort to locate the father.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 索逋而来，逋偿而去 - Metaphorical expression that can be traced back to classical Chinese literature, often referring to fulfilling a debt or obligation.
  • 飘然远引 - Commonly used in classical Chinese to describe someone who leaves lightly and far away, often with a sense of ease or detachment.
  • 五子也 - Naming of the sons follows the traditional Chinese practice of naming children in order of birth, with specific characters chosen for their meanings.
  • 皆颖敏，早入泮 - Refers to the ancient Chinese school, indicating that the sons started their education at a young age, which was highly valued in Chinese culture.
  • 琅补邑明经 - High-ranking examination in the imperial examination system, indicating a significant academic achievement.
  • 环珊均乡魁 - Winning the top honor in a local competition, which was a prestigious achievement.
  • 琥中明通榜进士 - Highest degree in the imperial examination system, indicating a high-ranking academic success.
  • 玖食廪饩 - Stipend provided by the government, often to scholars or officials.
  • 太母四，均长斋绣佛 - Maintaining a vegetarian diet and embroidering Buddha statues, common religious practices in Chinese Buddhism.
  • 委家政于妇 - Traditional Chinese practice of entrusting household affairs to women, particularly in older generations.
  • 大太母寿八十五 - Mention of the grandmother's age is a common way to express respect and longevity in Chinese culture.
  • 孙环视于庭，嫠泫然 - Reflects the emotional and familial dynamics in Chinese culture, where the loss of a parent can be deeply felt.

The phrase means that the encounters in Anhui, Shandong, and the local area were all part of the same day and time.

  • 莫不饮佩，曰：“此由儒而丐而仙，天台之花孝廉也。” - 莫不饮佩 means everyone was impressed, 此由儒而丐而仙 describes the transformation from a scholar to a beggar to a sage, and 天台之花孝廉 refers to a specific person, Hu Hualian from TianTai. This phrase indicates that everyone was impressed and commented on the transformation.
  • 而孝廉远矣！ - 而 means but, 孝廉 refers to a scholar-official, and 远矣 means far away. This phrase expresses that the scholar-official is far away.
  • 懊侬氏曰：花孝廉一瓶一钵，云水往来 - 懊侬氏 is a pseudonym, 一瓶一钵 means a bottle and a bowl, and 云水往来 means traveling like clouds and water. This phrase describes Hu Hualian's simple and wandering lifestyle.
  • 当其撒手悬崖，视妻子如敝屐 - 撒手悬崖 means to let go of the cliff, and 视妻子如敝屐 means to view one's wife and children as old shoes. This phrase describes Hu Hualian's detachment from his family.
  • 亦何其忍哉！ - 亦何其忍哉 means how cruel it is. This phrase expresses the cruelty of Hu Hualian's actions.
  • 或别有慧根，知满场袍笏，终有散场时耳 - 或别有慧根 means perhaps he had a different kind of wisdom, 知满场袍笏 means knowing that the stage of officials and scholars is full, and 终有散场时耳 means there will come a time when the show ends. This phrase suggests that Hu Hualian might have had a deeper understanding of the impermanence of worldly status.
  • 尝谓富且贵者，人视之，诚神仙不若 - 尝谓 means once said, 富且贵者 refers to the rich and powerful, and 诚神仙不若 means truly not as good as immortals. This phrase expresses that the rich and powerful are not as enviable as immortals.
  • 己视之，亦南面不易 - 己视之 means to look at it from one's own perspective, and 亦南面不易 means it is also not easy to sit in the south (a metaphor for being in power). This phrase indicates that even for the rich and powerful, maintaining their status is not easy.
  • 而仙人视之，则桎梏其身，毒其性，不过成一可怜虫 - 仙人视之 means immortals see it, 桎梏其身 means to bind their bodies, 毒其性 means to poison their nature, and 不过成一可怜虫 means they become nothing more than pitiful creatures. This phrase describes how immortals view the rich and powerful.
  • 乃彼不自怜，犹津津骂花孝廉，曰：“是不肖薄福儿也，是无知怪诞人也。” - 乃彼不自怜 means they do not pity themselves, 犹津津骂 means they still talk about it eagerly, and 是不肖薄福儿也，是无知怪诞人也 means they are unworthy and have little fortune, and they are ignorant and eccentric. This phrase describes how the rich and powerful criticize Hu Hualian.
  • 噫，愚哉！ - 噫 is an interjection expressing disappointment, and 愚哉 means foolish. This phrase expresses the author's disappointment in the rich and powerful.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 女子缠足 - The practice of foot binding, which was prevalent in China from the Song Dynasty (960–1279) to the early 20th century. It involved tightly binding the feet of young girls to prevent them from growing, resulting in small, deformed feet that were considered a sign of beauty and status.
  • 东昏侯 - Reference to Emperor Wu of Southern Qi (萧宝卷, 483–501), who was known for his debauchery and cruelty. The term '寡耻鲜廉' (lacking shame and integrity) is used to criticize his moral failings.
  • 莲花 - In Chinese culture, the lotus flower is a symbol of purity and grace, often associated with Buddhist and Taoist traditions. Here, it is used ironically to contrast the beauty of the bound feet with the pain and suffering they cause.
  • 粉黛 - Term often used to refer to women or their makeup. It is derived from the words '粉' (powder) and '黛' (eyebrow pencil), both of which were used in ancient Chinese beauty practices.
  • 英气 - Term that describes a person with a strong, heroic, or martial spirit. It is often used to describe men, but here it is applied to a woman, suggesting a departure from traditional gender roles.
  • 破产以购之 - Hyperbolic expression that emphasizes the extreme measures one might take to obtain something. It reflects the high value placed on certain qualities or abilities in Chinese culture.
  • 一捧砚，一添香，一负剑 - These actions represent different aspects of traditional Chinese culture. '捧砚' is associated with scholarly pursuits, '添香' with religious or spiritual practices, and '负剑' with martial arts or military service.
  • 吾乡挈东鄙人 - Reference to someone from the author's hometown who is described as '东鄙人,' which can mean 'a person from the eastern border' or 'a person from a remote area.' This suggests that the person is from a less prestigious or distant region.
  • 自恃足大善走，难将及 - Statement describing the person's confidence in their ability to run quickly due to having large feet. This reflects the cultural value placed on physical agility and speed.
  • 先嘱良人挈子女潜遁 - Description of the person's actions before a crisis, where they instruct their husband to take the children and flee. This reflects the traditional role of women in Chinese society, where they were often expected to protect their families in times of danger.
  • 己则摒挡长物 - Description of the person's decision to leave behind their belongings. This reflects the practical and sometimes selfless nature of women in Chinese culture, who were often willing to sacrifice their possessions for the sake of their families.

  • 然四顾荒郊，无一树一石，可以揽辔 - 然四顾荒郊 means 'but looking around the desolate countryside,' 无一树一石 means 'there was not a single tree or rock,' and 可以揽辔 means 'where he could hold the reins.' This suggests that the bandit looked around and found no trees or rocks where he could hold the reins.

  • 贼求计甚急，女大声曰 - 贼求计甚急 means 'the bandit urgently sought a plan,' and 女大声曰 means 'the woman shouted loudly.'
  • 急煞儿，盍以缰系于两足乎 - 急煞儿 means 'you're in a hurry,' and 盍以缰系于两足乎 means 'why not tie the reins to your feet?' This suggests that the woman suggested tying the reins to the bandit's feet.
  • 贼抚掌称善，乃弯腰俯首，牢缚不少松 - 贼抚掌称善 means 'the bandit clapped and praised it,' 乃弯腰俯首 means 'then bent down,' and 牢缚不少松 means 'tied tightly without loosening.' This indicates that the bandit clapped and praised the idea, then bent down and tied the reins tightly without loosening them.

  • 黄坯当甓 - '黄坯' refers to yellow clay bricks, and '甓' is tile or brick. This phrase likely means using rough or unrefined bricks as substitutes for proper tiles.
  • 铺牡蛎之路 - '牡蛎' is oyster. This phrase may be metaphorical or literal, suggesting a path paved with oyster shells, possibly indicating a rough or natural landscape.

  • 槽头喂板角之青 - 'Feeding the horned green at the manger,' likely referring to feeding a young or horned animal (such as a bull or ox) with green fodder.
  • 力能耕地 - 'Strong enough to plow fields,' indicating the animal's strength.
  • 门前拴粉嘴之白 - 'Tying up the white with a pink mouth at the gate,' likely referring to a white animal (such as a cow or horse) with a pink mouth, possibly a calf or young animal.
  • 喊可惊天 - 'Its cry can startle heaven,' a metaphorical way of saying the animal is strong and loud.
  • 徒守清贫 - 'To merely hold onto a life of poverty,' indicating a life of simplicity or austerity.

  • 身穿四块瓦 - 'Wearing four pieces of tile as clothing,' a humorous or metaphorical way of saying one is dressed in a very simple or patched-up way.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 驴化为履 - A metaphorical phrase meaning 'a donkey transforms into shoes.' Likely a play on words or a riddle, as '驴' (donkey) and '履' (shoes) sound similar in Chinese.
  • 拥厚资，而悭吝殊甚 - Means 'possesses great wealth but is extremely stingy,' highlighting the character's greed.
  • 体患疥 - Means 'suffers from scabies,' a skin condition, used metaphorically to describe the man's repulsive nature.
  • 与人较锱铢 - Means 'argues over the smallest details,' emphasizing his miserliness.
  • 恒狡赖 - Means 'always deceives and denies,' further characterizing his dishonesty.
  • 为子延师 - Means 'hires a tutor for his son,' indicating his concern for his child's education despite his own greed.
  • 馆于家 - Means 'teaches at home,' suggesting a private tutoring arrangement.
  • 多以冷字问师 - Means 'often asks the tutor difficult or obscure questions,' implying that he tests the tutor's knowledge.
  • 师略嗫嚅，即云不通，摄揄之 - Means 'the tutor hesitates and says he doesn't understand, so he ridicules him,' showing the tutor's frustration and the man's harsh treatment.
  • 人多裹足不敢就邑 - Means 'many people hesitate to teach in his town,' indicating the negative reputation he has earned.
  • 邑佟生，滑稽士也 - 'The townsman Tong is a witty and humorous scholar,' introducing a character who contrasts with the greedy man.
  • 贫无已，俯就其馆 - Means 'poor and unable to refuse, he accepts the tutoring position,' showing the tutor's financial desperation.
  • 甫莅皋比 - Means 'as soon as he arrived at the teaching position,' indicating the start of his tenure.
  • 即大书一“”字于壁下 - Means 'he immediately wrote a large blank space on the wall,' which is likely a riddle or puzzle.
  • 注云：“人能识得，方许以冷字问我。” - Means 'noted: 'Only if someone can identify it will I allow them to ask me difficult questions.'' This suggests a challenge or test.
  • 翁遍搜六书八法，广询名儒硕彦，不可得其音 - Means 'the old man searched through all six scripts and eight methods, and widely consulted famous scholars, but could not find its meaning,' indicating the complexity of the riddle.
  • 婉询于生，笑不答 - Means 'he gently asked the scholar, who smiled and did not reply,' showing the scholar's nonchalant response.
  • 翁于市上购物，必精择其价之廉者 - Means 'the old man always carefully selects the cheapest items when shopping,' emphasizing his greed.
  • 买鱼为膳，非腐败不入门 - Means 'he buys fish for meals, only accepting rotten ones,' further illustrating his miserliness.
  • 人问之，曰：“吾恐伤生耳。” - Means 'when asked, he says, 'I'm afraid of harming the fish's life,' a hypocritical excuse for his behavior.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This long sequence describes a series of actions: using a butcher's knife to sever the donkey's head, the blood dripping onto the street market, the meat being sliced and hung on the wall, and people quickly buying it.
  • This clause describes the old man's reaction to the event: he heard about it, hurriedly took money, and bought all the remaining meat.
  • This clause describes the guests' reaction: they pooled money to buy the meat, received ten units of money, and went away on foot.
  • This clause describes the old man's actions upon returning home: he salted the donkey meat, stored it, cut it into small pieces, cooked it, and rejoiced.
  • This clause describes the kitchen maid's actions: she lit the fire to cook, after some time, accidentally lifted the lid of the pot, looked at the doneness, was greatly surprised, and found the pot suddenly turned into a pair of rotten straw shoes.
  • This clause describes the old man's reaction: he was greatly shocked, looked into the jar, and found it full of pairs of straw shoes.
  • This clause describes the old man's inquiry: he asked the neighbors who had sold meat, and they also had the same experience.
  • This clause explains the reason: the neighbors sold less meat, not as much as the old man, likely because it was a wandering magician using a trick of misdirection to break into the miser's purse, which the old man did not know.
  • This clause describes the old man's final reaction: he felt ashamed and regretful, and shouted in anger, again inexpressible.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昙花本佛国产 - Literal phrase meaning 'the昙花 originally comes from the land of Buddha,' used to describe the origin of the昙花.

  • 生自在香 - Means 'produces a natural fragrance,' highlighting the inherent beauty and purity of the昙花.
  • 婆娑而舞 - Describes the graceful movement of the昙花 when it hears the梵呗声 (sacred chanting).
  • 朝开夕落 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'opens in the morning and falls in the evening,' indicating the昙花's short lifespan.
  • 赋命不长 - Means 'has a short lifespan,' emphasizing the昙花's brief existence.
  • 佛祖慈悲，见之泪下 - Describes the Buddha's compassion, implying that even the Buddha is moved to tears upon seeing the昙花.
  • 昔太史戴公督学西秦时 - Historical reference to a past event where a scholar named Dai supervised education in the western region of Qin.
  • 辟门唱名 - Means 'opened the door and called out names,' describing the process of calling students to take exams.
  • 有七龄章童子名节 - Refers to a seven-year-old child named Jie, who is described as having a distinguished appearance.
  • 丫髻缠红丝 - Describes the child's hairstyle, with his hair tied in a topknot and wrapped in red threads.
  • 面如冠玉 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'face like a jade crown,' describing the child's beautiful and refined appearance.
  • 提笔囊登阶接卷 - Means 'carried his writing bag and ascended the steps to receive the exam paper,' indicating the child's readiness to take the exam.
  • 太史藐其稚 - Means 'the scholar looked down on his youth,' suggesting that the scholar underestimated the child's abilities due to his young age.
  • 咄！节院乃文战之地，非婴孩跳荡之区，汝来此何为？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'Hmph! This is a place for scholarly combat, not a playground for infants. What are you doing here?' The scholar questions the child's presence.
  • 揖而对曰 - Means 'he bowed and replied,' indicating the child's respectful response.
  • 童子无知，观光有志 - The child's reply, meaning 'I am just a child, but I have the ambition to observe and learn.'
  • 汝能作文乎？若块然没字碑，当以夏楚惩汝 - The scholar's challenge, meaning 'Can you write an essay? If you are a blank stone without any writing, I will punish you with a stick.'
  • 虽未敢径夺锦标，亦未必遽撄扑教 - The child's response, meaning 'Although I may not dare to win the championship outright, I also do not immediately expect to be punished.'
  • 太史颇以为夸 - Means 'the scholar found this quite boastful,' indicating that the scholar was impressed by the child's confidence.

  • 耐姑年六十，出资为雨香庵建佛塔，报大悟也 - Means 'Nai's wife, at sixty years old, funded the construction of a Buddha stupa at the Yu Xiang Temple to repay the great sage.'

  • 吾愿不识字人，其胸无点墨而腹有疑团者，当三复此编 - Means 'I wish for those who do not know how to read, who have no ink in their hearts but many doubts in their bellies, to read this text three times.'

  • 翁忘却耶？仆盗资若干，僮盗资若干，日食柴米，翁病药饵又若干，舟子支付船值又若干 - Detailed account meaning 'Have you forgotten? The servants stole so much, the boy stole so much, the daily expenses for food and firewood, your medicine for illness, and the boatman's payment for the ship,' listing financial losses.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吊汨罗之万丈兮，臣心同清 - Poetic expression meaning 'Mourning the deep waters of Miluo River, my heart is as clear as it.'

  • 某请和以巴里歌 - Formal expression meaning 'I will respond with a *Bali* song.'

  • 群起酌以巨觥曰 - Formal expression meaning 'All raise large cups and say.'

  • 视美人无一言 - Describes a situation where the beautiful woman remains silent.

  • 对曰：“请以珊管瑶笺，书二十八字，命乐工歌之，何如？” - Formal expression meaning 'She replied, 'Please use a jade flute and a precious scroll, write twenty-eight characters, and have the musicians sing it, how about that?''

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 珊瑚树真有高六七尺者 - Literal description of coral trees being six or seven feet tall, which is unusual and thus noteworthy.
  • 人谓售之碧眼贾，富可跨石崇 - Colloquial expression where '碧眼贾' (green-eyed merchant) is a term for foreigners, and '石崇' is a historical figure known for his wealth. The phrase suggests that selling the coral to a foreign merchant could make someone as rich as Shi Chong.
  • 吾不能为龙宫之贼 - Humorous self-deprecating statement, implying that the speaker does not want to be seen as a thief from the dragon palace, a place of great wealth and power.
  • 乘观者不意，悉举而投之 - The speaker takes advantage of the audience's lack of expectation to throw everything away, a dramatic and unexpected action.
  • 世子悬弧之辰，遣介士招饮耳 - Formal and somewhat archaic way of saying that the prince (世子) is celebrating a significant event (悬弧之辰, the first birthday of a son) and has sent an envoy to invite the speaker to a feast.
  • 众谋攫其宝 - Colloquial expression indicating that the crowd plans to seize the treasure.
  • 大郎惊醒，惧失信于世子 - The speaker (大郎) wakes up in a panic, fearing he will break his promise to the prince.
  • 遂鲠而死 - Dramatic and somewhat archaic way of saying that the speaker choked to death.
  • 乡人怜之，殓以桐棺，海畔 - The locals show compassion by burying the speaker in a simple coffin near the sea.
  • 是夕风雷震震，翌已成一巨坟 - Poetic description of a storm that night, followed by the formation of a large mound the next day.
  • 明年海涨，复于坟下拥一沙岗 - The sea rises the following year, creating a sandbar around the grave.
  • 而潮亦至此止 - The tide stops at this point, suggesting a natural boundary.
  • 死后且时著灵异，乡民即坟为祠 - After death, the speaker becomes associated with miraculous occurrences, and the locals build a shrine at the grave.
  • 百里外浏河，素多海患，居民避波臣者纷然 - Description of a distant river (浏河) that is often affected by sea disasters, causing residents to flee.
  • 时梦一神人，卤薄赫赫，冠冕峨峨，自云：“我石大郎也，悯此处没于海，急迁棺，或可免厄 - Dream vision of a divine figure who claims to be the deceased (石大郎) and warns of an impending disaster, urging the relocation of the coffin to prevent calamity.
